entry.title: Building Durable AI Agents - Announcing the `cds-langgraph-persistence` Plugin
entry.content:
For the past year, I’ve been experimenting and building AI agents with LangChain/LangGraph inside the CAP Node.js runtime on SAP BTP. This has been less about “toy agents” and more about understanding what it actually takes to run agents reliably in SAP landscapes, next to real business systems and users.

When you look at AI agents as a software architecture (not just “LLM + tools”) and are planning to take it to production, you face the critical requirements: identity, authn/authz, multitenancy, persistence, event‑driven flows, scalability, observability, CI/CD, and so on. CAP already solves most of this for applications on BTP, which is why I’m increasingly convinced that “LangChain/LangGraph + CAP Node.js” is one of the best combinations for taking agents to production in SAP environments. I’ll share a more detailed take on that combo in a separate post.

In this post, let’s narrow the scope and focus on one foundational capability: durability — the ability for an agent to maintain state, survive failures, and resume multi‑step, long‑running tasks without losing its context. We’ll see how LangGraph’s checkpointing model works, and how a CAP‑native LangGraph/LangChain Checkpointer (via @mi8y/cds-langgraph-persistence) lets you bring durable agents to SAP BTP with minimal friction.

Thinking of agents as state machines

A useful mental model is to treat an AI agent as a "state machine." At any point in time, the agent has a “state”: the current conversation history, internal variables, partial results, tool outputs, and whatever else it needs to decide the next step in its workflow.

AI Agent as State Graph

As the agent works towards a goal, this "state" changes: new messages are added, tools are called, intermediate decisions are made. If you could take a snapshot of that state on every transition and store it in durable persistence, you would be able to:

  • Recover from crashes or infra failures by reloading the latest snapshot.
  • Resume long‑running workflows after delays (e.g. waiting for human approval).
  • Inspect what the agent did, step by step.
  • “Time‑travel” back to a previous checkpoint if needed. (useful for forking or replaying in case of an UI trying to catch up with a long-running agent)

This is exactly what LangGraph’s checkpointers are about: they provide a persistence layer that saves a snapshot of the graph’s state on each step and make it retrievable later, keyed by a thread or workflow id.

What are LangGraph Checkpoints?

LangGraph offers a checkpointing interface that you plug into your agent graph when you compile it. Each time the graph advances, the checkpointer records a snapshot of the state. These snapshots are stored somewhere (in‑memory or in a database), and referenced by a thread_id or similar key so you can resume or inspect a specific conversation. Please note that LangGraph, LangChain and DeepAgents all share the same checkpointing interface, so you can swap implementations without changing your agent logic.

Checkpointing in LangGraph

This unlocks

  • Conversational memory: the agent remembers previous turns without you manually managing history.
  • Fault tolerance: if the app restarts, you can reload the last checkpoint and continue.
  • Time travel and introspection: you can inspect or replay previous states.
  • Human‑in‑the‑loop: you can pause at a checkpoint, wait for human input, and resume from there.

The simplest way to get started is the in‑memory checkpointer that LangGraph ships with:

import { MemorySaver } from "@langchain/langgraph";

const checkpointer = new MemorySaver(); // in-memory; good for dev, loses state on restart

const agent = createAgent({
  checkpointer, // just pass the instance
  // other settings: model, tools, etc.
});

This is perfect for local experimentation and debugging, but it only keeps checkpoints in memory. Once your CAP app restarts or scales, those checkpoints are gone. For SAP BTP and CAP, you need something that speaks to your actual persistence layer and fits into your multi‑tenant, production setup.

Why a CAP/CDS‑native checkpointer?

Out of the box, LangGraph/LangChain provide checkpointer implementations for stores like SQLite (good for local dev), Postgres, Redis and other generic backends for production. In a typical non‑SAP BTP environment, that’s often enough.

On SAP BTP with CAP and SAP HANA Cloud, things are slightly more nuanced:

  • You might be using HDI containers per tenant, with separate schemas.
  • You might just have a single HANA database
  • You need agents to run locally for dev and in CI pipelines for agent evals without heavyweight setup.
  • You don’t want to bolt on an entirely different persistence stack just for agent state, bypassing CAP’s data model and lifecycle.

To solve this, I'm announcing an NPM package - @mi8y/cds-langgraph-persistence. This combines two roles:

  • A CDS plugin that wires the required persistence entities into your CAP data model.
  • A LangGraph/LangChain checkpointer adapter that uses those entities to store and retrieve checkpoints.

This means:

  • You stay within CAP’s multitenancy and lifecycle model.
  • You don’t need to manually create or deploy tables for checkpoints.
  • The same code works locally, in CI, and on SAP BTP, as long as CAP can talk to the configured database.

The code is opensource and MIT licensed. The package is designed to work with CAP v9 and the latest v10, and acts as a drop‑in replacement for any checkpointer you might already be using.

Here’s what swapping looks like at the agent level:

import { CdsCheckpointSaver } from "@mi8y/cds-langgraph-persistence";

- const checkpointer = new MemorySaver(); // any non-CAP checkpointer
+ const checkpointer = new CdsCheckpointSaver({
+   name: "my-agent",
+ });

const agent = createAgent({
  checkpointer,
  // ...
});

Internally, the CDS plugin injects the necessary entities into your model, while CAP takes care of tenant routing, schema management, and lifecycle. You get durability and multitenancy “for free”, without custom plumbing.

Multi‑tenancy and lifecycle, the CAP way

Because the plugin adds entities directly into your CDS model, CAP treats checkpoint data like any other CAP‑managed entity:

  • Multitenancy: if your CAP app is configured for multitenancy, CAP routes checkpoint reads/writes to the correct tenant container based on the request context. You don’t have to manually add tenant IDs to agent state or build your own sharding logic.
  • Lifecycle: you don’t need to deploy checkpoint tables separately. CAP’s usual deployment flow handles entity lifecycle, whether you’re using SQLite, HANA, Postgres or any custom DB adapter during development and production.

Getting started in a CAP project

Let’s walk through a minimal example of wiring a LangGraph agent with CAP and the CDS checkpointer.

1. Install the package

In your CAP Node.js project:

npm install @mi8y/cds-langgraph-persistence

2. Add CDS entities

Run the following command to add the necessary CDS entities

cds add langgraph-persistence

3. Define a simple agent service

Expose a REST action that will drive the agent, passing in a threadId and the latest user message:

@protocol: 'rest'
service AgentService {
  action invoke(threadId: String, content: String) returns String;
}

4. Add a custom CDS handler with the checkpointer

In your service implementation, initialize the checkpointer and agent once, then use them inside the action handler:

import cds from "@sap/cds";
import { CdsCheckpointSaver } from "@mi8y/cds-langgraph-persistence";
import { createAgent } from "./agent"; // your LangGraph agent factory

export class AgentService extends cds.ApplicationService {
  init() {
    // Initialize a CAP-aware checkpointer for an agent named "my-agent"
    const checkpointSaver = new CdsCheckpointSaver({ name: "my-agent" });

    const agent = createAgent({
      checkpointer: checkpointSaver,
      // other configuration: model, system prompt, tools, etc.
    });

    this.on("invoke", async (req) => {
      const { threadId, content } = req.data;

      // Invoke the agent with just the latest user message
      const res = await agent.invoke(
        { messages: [{ role: "user", content }] },
        {
          configurable: {
            // Use a unique threadId per user to isolate conversation threads
            thread_id: `${threadId}-${req.user.id}`,
          },
        }
      );

      // Return the last message content from the agent
      return res.messages[res.messages.length - 1].content;
    });

    return super.init();
  }
}

A few important points:

  • You only pass the latest user message into the agent. You don’t manually maintain full history in your service code. Checkpointer handles that for you.
  • The thread_id inside configurable is the key that LangGraph uses to group checkpoints. Combining threadId with req.user.id gives you per‑user, per‑thread isolation.
  • The checkpointer persists each step of the graph for that thread_id into CAP’s persistence. When the next request comes in with the same threadId, the agent resumes with full context.

5. Invoke the agent and see durability in action

With the service deployed, you can start a conversation:

POST /rest/agent/invoke
Content-Type: application/json

{
  "threadId": "test-thread-1",
  "content": "Give me the list of all the books in the catalog."
}

Then, continue the conversation with the same threadId:

POST /rest/agent/invoke
Content-Type: application/json

{
  "threadId": "test-thread-1",
  "content": "What questions have I asked you so far?"
}

Even though you only send the latest user message, the agent can recall the previous turns because the checkpointer has been saving and loading the full state behind the scenes.

To test isolation, try a different threadId:

POST /rest/agent/invoke
Content-Type: application/json

{
  "threadId": "test-thread-2",
  "content": "What questions have I asked you so far?"
}

This should behave like a fresh conversation, since it uses a different thread and therefore a different checkpoint stream.

If you want to see a complete example, including the agent definition and tools, you can look at the LangChain example in the GitHub repo: examples/langchain-cds-persistence.

Practical best practices for durable agents on CAP

Once you have durability, a few patterns help keep things sane in production:

  • Use meaningful threadId values
    Treat threadId as a business‑level concept: a conversation, a workflow instance, a support ticket, etc. Don’t just generate random IDs with no semantic meaning.

  • Let CAP own multitenancy
    The package injects entities into your CDS model, and CAP handles tenant routing. Avoid building custom tenant switches inside your agent logic; rely on CAP’s standard patterns instead.

  • Plan checkpoint lifecycle
    Checkpoints will accumulate over time. You should periodically clean up old threads that are no longer active. For example:

    const staleThreads = await tx.run(
  SELECT.from(Checkpoints)
    .columns("graphName", "namespace", "threadId", "max(createdAt) as lastActivity")
    .groupBy("graphName", "namespace", "threadId")
    .having("max(createdAt) <", cutoffTime)
);

    You can then delete checkpoints for those stale threads, or archive them depending on your compliance needs.

  • Expose entities for debugging (optionally)
    For troubleshooting and introspection, you can project the plugin’s entities into your own service:

    using plugin.langgraph.persistence as langgraph from '@mi8y/cds-langgraph-persistence';

service CheckpointViewerService {
  entity Checkpoints as projection on langgraph.Checkpoints; // view all checkpoints
}

    This is handy during development or incident analysis, but you don’t need to expose such viewers in production if you have other observability tooling.

Durability is just one piece of building production‑ready agents. You still need observability, evals, guardrails, and human‑in‑the‑loop patterns that fit your SAP landscape. The goal of @mi8y/cds-langgraph-persistence is to make the durability part boring and reliable. In the future blogs, I'll cover other aspects of taking agents to production.

  • GitHub repo: https://github.com/mi8y/cds-langgraph-persistence
  • NPM package: @mi8y/cds-langgraph-persistence

Disclaimer: This article was written by me, but the narrative refined and type-checked by AI.
Edit 1: Added cds add langgraph-persistence to import the CDS entities

entry.title: From Preview to Production: Get Started with the SAP HANA Cloud's Agentic Data Exploration Tools
entry.content:
At SAP TechEd 2025, we shared a vision: what if exploring data felt less like archaeology and more like a conversation? We introduced two capabilities designed to make that real and the response told us we were onto something. Fast forward to today. That vision is now in production. SAP HANA Cloud's data exploration capabilities are generally available, and customers can start using them right now.

If you followed the TechEd announcement, you will remember these capabilities as the Discovery Agent and the Data Agent. As they matured from prototype to production, so did our thinking about what to call them.

They are now formally known as the Database Object Discovery Tool and the Data Retrieval Tool - together, the SAP HANA Cloud data exploration tools. The rename reflects a natural evolution: as these capabilities became production-grade, composable building blocks that any agent, application, or workflow can invoke, the "tool" framing became the more precise one. The underlying power is the same. 

Note: **Upcoming Agents. Subject to change as we evolve our roadmap

Why This Matters - For Everyone

Whether you are a developer building an AI-powered application, or a business stakeholder who simply wants answers from data, these tools close a gap that has existed for a long time.

For business users and decision makers:

  • You no longer need to know where data lives to find it. The Database Object Discovery Tool searches across tables, views, and relationships based on what you describe, or query about.
  • You no longer need SQL to get results. The Data Retrieval Tool translates a plain language request into a query and returns the answer, no manual lookup, no schema expertise required.
  • The experience feels conversational, even though sophisticated discovery and query generation is happening beneath the surface.

For developers and architects:

  • Production-grade interfaces: stored-procedure-based (AI_OBJECT_DISCOVERY and AI_DATA_RETRIEVAL) that integrate cleanly into any application or agent workflow.
  • Composable by design: wire them into an MCP server or call them directly from your own tooling & agents.
  • Context inherited automatically: the Custom Database Objects Knowledge Graph continues to serve as the semantic backbone, so your applications gain that contextual understanding without you having to build it.

Getting Started

There is no additional infrastructure to provision. If you have access to SAP HANA Cloud with the Natural Language Processing & Triple Store services enabled, and an SAP AI Core subscription, you have access to these tools today.

The pattern is straightforward:

  1. Discover - Describe what you are looking for in natural language. The Database Object Discovery Tool searches the HANA catalog's knowledge graph & RAG layer and returns the relevant tables, views, and columns.
  2. Retrieve - Pass that context to the Data Retrieval Tool along with your question. It generates and executes the appropriate query and returns results.

Try It Yourself -  Explore Data with SAP HANA Cloud Data Exploration Tools

The SAP Developer Tutorials has a step by step guide that walks you through setting up both tools and running your first natural-language query against SAP HANA Cloud. It covers the key features that make HANA Cloud well-suited for business AI, and gets you hands-on quickly.

SAP HANA Cloud Central Tooling

 

What's Next: Taking the Tools Further with MCP
 
These tools are built to be composable and one of the most powerful ways to use them is through the Model Context Protocol (MCP). In the next post in this series, we will walk through how to spin up a local MCP client that connects directly to the Database Object Discovery Tool and the Data Retrieval Tool, and what that unlocks for agentic AI workflows on top of SAP HANA Cloud.
If you have been curious about how SAP HANA Cloud fits into the broader agentic architecture picture at the protocol level that one is for you.
The Data Exploration Tools are more than a new capability, they mark SAP HANA Cloud's evolution toward an agentic database that not only manages data, but also enables users and AI agents to understand, explore, and leverage it intelligently. This is the direction SAP HANA Cloud is moving and there is more to come.

entry.title: MCP Server for SAP Incentive Management
entry.content:
Disclaimer

The views and ideas expressed in this blog are strictly my personal views and were built in my personal capacity. This blog does not represent, involve, or reflect the views, architecture, data, systems, security policies, or implementation approach of any organization I currently work for or have worked for in the past.

This blog is intended only as a technical exploration and proof of concept. Any implementation involving SAP Incentive Management, SAP Advanced Workflow, customer environments, backend databases, APIs, AI agents, or MCP servers must be reviewed and approved according to the applicable customer policies, SAP terms, security requirements, data privacy rules, and enterprise architecture standards before being built or used.

Introduction

Enterprise incentive compensation platforms often contain a large amount of operational and analytical data: participants, transactions, credits, earnings, deposits, periods, processing results, and exception scenarios. When a support engineer, consultant, or analyst investigates an issue, the work usually starts with questions such as:

  • Has the transaction data loaded for a participant?
  • Are credits or earnings generated for a given period?
  • What records exist for a specific order, payee, or processing run?
  • Can the result be summarized without manually writing the same SQL repeatedly?

With the rise of AI agents and tool-based reasoning, I wanted to explore whether a natural language interface could help simplify this investigation pattern. This blog presents a personal proof of concept for connecting an MCP server to an SAP Incentive Management backend database, specifically for an Oracle-based application environment, using SAP Advanced Workflow as the controlled access layer.

Core idea: a user asks a data question in natural language, an MCP client invokes a tool, the MCP server calls an SAP Advanced Workflow Web Action, the Web Action performs controlled backend access, and the result is returned to the AI client for tabular display, summarization, or charting.

What Is MCP?

MCP stands for Model Context Protocol. It is an open protocol that allows AI applications to connect with external tools, systems, and data sources through a standardized client-server model. In MCP, servers expose tools or resources, and AI clients can invoke those tools when needed to answer user questions or perform tasks.

At a high level, MCP includes:

  • Host: the AI application or assistant.
  • Client: the connector inside the host that communicates with an MCP server.
  • Server: the service that exposes tools, data, resources, or actions.
  • Tools: functions that the AI system can call.
  • Resources: contextual data exposed by the server.

Why SAP Incentive Management?

SAP Incentive Management is commonly used to support complex incentive compensation processes, including sales transactions, credits, earnings, payments, disputes, and performance insights. In such systems, data investigation is a recurring activity. A conversational interface can be useful if it is designed carefully, especially for read-only support and analysis scenarios.

Example user questions:

  • Show transactions for participant 1001 for January 2026.
  • Summarize credit records by period.
  • Find failed transaction records for the latest processing period.
  • Display earning results for a participant and explain the output.

Why SAP Advanced Workflow?

SAP Advanced Workflow provides Web Action scripts that can receive external HTTP requests in JSON or XML format and execute workflow-side logic. This makes it an interesting controlled entry point for a proof of concept.

Instead of giving the MCP server direct database connectivity, the pattern I explored uses Advanced Workflow as an intermediate execution layer:

CLI / MCP Client / Conversational AI
        ↓
MCP Server
        ↓
HTTP API Call
        ↓
SAP Advanced Workflow Web Action Script
        ↓
Controlled DB Access Logic
        ↓
SAP Incentive Management Backend Database
        ↓
Result Returned to Advanced Workflow
        ↓
Response Returned to MCP Server
        ↓
AI Agent Formats, Reasons, and Displays Output

This separation matters. The MCP server should not become an uncontrolled database gateway. Advanced Workflow can act as a central place to validate request payloads, enforce allowed operations, and return structured responses.

Proof-of-Concept Scope

For the first version, I kept the implementation intentionally small. The MCP server exposes:

  • A Streamable HTTP MCP endpoint at /mcp.
  • A health endpoint at /health.
  • One read-only MCP tool named select_query.

The initial tool accepts a SQL query and a maximum row count:

{
  "query": "SELECT ...",
  "maxRows": 100
}

The server applies basic read-only restrictions before calling the SAP Advanced Workflow Web Action API:

  • Only SELECT or WITH queries are accepted.
  • Non-read keywords such as INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, and DROP are blocked.
  • Multiple statements are blocked.
  • The returned rows are capped using maxRows.

Important Design Warning

For a proof of concept, a read-only select_query tool is useful to validate the integration flow. For a production-grade design, I would not recommend exposing free-form SQL to an AI client, even with keyword blocking. A safer design is to expose a catalog of predefined query types such as participant_transactions, credit_summary, or earning_results, and let the Web Action map those query types to approved SQL statements.

MCP Server and Tool Definition

The MCP server is implemented in Node.js using the MCP SDK. For the blog, I am not including the full server implementation. The core pieces that matter are:

  • The MCP server identity and declared tool capability.
  • The select_query tool definition.
  • The tool request handler that validates input, sanitizes the SQL, calls SAP Advanced Workflow, and returns structured content.

Tool Name and Input Schema

The proof of concept exposes one MCP tool named select_query. The input schema requires a SQL query and optionally accepts a maximum row count.

const SELECT_QUERY_TOOL = "select_query";

const selectQueryInputSchema = z.object({
  query: z.string().min(1).max(10000),
  maxRows: z.number().int().positive().max(5000).optional(),
});

MCP Tool Definition

The tool is described as a read-only operation. The annotations are important because they tell the MCP client that this tool is intended for read-only use and should not be treated as a destructive action.

const tools: Tool[] = [
  {
    name: "select_query",
    title: "Run SELECT query via SAP AWF",
    description:
      "Runs a read-only SQL SELECT query by calling an SAP Advanced Workflow web action endpoint.",
    inputSchema: {
      type: "object",
      properties: {
        query: {
          type: "string",
          description: "Read-only SQL query. Only SELECT/WITH statements are allowed.",
        },
        maxRows: {
          type: "number",
          description: "Optional max rows returned by MCP response.",
        },
      },
      required: ["query"],
      additionalProperties: false,
    },
    annotations: {
      destructiveHint: false,
      openWorldHint: false,
      readOnlyHint: true,
    },
  },
];

MCP Server Definition

The server is registered with a name, version, and tool capability. It supports tool discovery through ListToolsRequestSchema and tool execution through CallToolRequestSchema.

function createMcpServer(): Server {
  const server = new Server(
    { name: "sap-awf-mcp-server", version: "1.0.0" },
    { capabilities: { tools: {} } }
  );

  server.setRequestHandler(ListToolsRequestSchema, async () => ({ tools }));

  server.setRequestHandler(CallToolRequestSchema, async (request: CallToolRequest) => {
    if (request.params.name !== SELECT_QUERY_TOOL) {
      throw new Error(`Unknown tool: ${request.params.name}`);
    }

    const parsedArgs = selectQueryInputSchema.parse(request.params.arguments ?? {});
    const safeQuery = sanitizeSelectQuery(parsedArgs.query);

    const queryResult = await runSelectViaAwf(safeQuery);
    const maxRows = parsedArgs.maxRows ?? appConfig.defaultMaxRows;
    const rows = queryResult.rows.slice(0, maxRows);
    const truncated = queryResult.rows.length > rows.length;

    return {
      content: [
        {
          type: "text",
          text: `Fetched ${rows.length} row(s). Source: SAP Advanced Workflow web action.`,
        },
      ],
      structuredContent: {
        rowCount: queryResult.rows.length,
        returnedRows: rows.length,
        truncated,
        rows,
      },
    };
  });

  return server;
}

SQL Sanitization Before Calling Advanced Workflow

Before sending the query to the Web Action endpoint, the MCP server removes comments, rejects multiple statements, allows only SELECT or WITH, and blocks obvious non-read operations.

function sanitizeSelectQuery(query: string): string {
  const withoutComments = query
    .trim()
    .replace(/--.*$/gm, "")
    .replace(/\/\*[\s\S]*?\*\//g, "")
    .trim();

  if (!withoutComments) {
    throw new Error("Query cannot be empty");
  }

  if ((withoutComments.match(/;/g) || []).length > 1) {
    throw new Error("Only one SQL statement is allowed");
  }

  const normalized = withoutComments.replace(/;\s*$/, "").trim();

  if (!/^(select|with)\b/i.test(normalized)) {
    throw new Error("Only SELECT/WITH queries are allowed");
  }

  const forbiddenOperations =
    /\b(insert|update|delete|merge|drop|alter|truncate|create|grant|revoke|commit|rollback|call|exec(?:ute)?|begin|declare)\b/i;

  if (forbiddenOperations.test(normalized)) {
    throw new Error("Only read-only SELECT queries are allowed");
  }

  return normalized;
}

Security Note on This MCP Design

This server-side validation is useful, but it is still not a complete security boundary. In a mature implementation, I would move from free-form SQL to predefined tools, query allowlists, database views, least-privilege datasource users, row limits, timeouts, audit logging, and field masking.

Configuration Overview

The MCP server uses environment-based configuration so that endpoint URLs and credentials are not hardcoded in source code.

PORT=3100
AWF_WEBACTION_URL=https://<your-awf-host>/api/webactions/<your-script-endpoint>
AWF_HTTP_METHOD=POST
AWF_QUERY_FIELD=query
AWF_TIMEOUT_MS=30000
AWF_ALLOW_INSECURE_TLS=true
DEFAULT_MAX_ROWS=500

# Auth options
# Use either bearer token OR basic auth (username/password), not both.
AWF_BEARER_TOKEN=
AWF_BASIC_USERNAME=<your-awf-username>
AWF_BASIC_PASSWORD=<your-awf-WS-token>
AWF_API_KEY=
AWF_API_KEY_HEADER=x-api-key

# Optional static JSON object merged into each outbound body
# AWF_STATIC_PAYLOAD_JSON={"tenant":"myTenant","script":"runSelect"}
AWF_STATIC_PAYLOAD_JSON=

Some useful fields:

  • AWF_WEBACTION_URL: the Advanced Workflow Web Action endpoint.
  • AWF_QUERY_FIELD: the JSON field expected by the Web Action script for the query.
  • AWF_STATIC_PAYLOAD_JSON: constant fields required by the Web Action script.
  • AWF_BEARER_TOKEN, AWF_BASIC_USERNAME, AWF_BASIC_PASSWORD, AWF_API_KEY: authentication options.
  • AWF_ALLOW_INSECURE_TLS: shown as true for this local proof-of-concept configuration. This should not be used for production unless explicitly approved by security and platform teams.

Advanced Workflow Web Action Script

The Web Action script acts as the controlled bridge between the MCP server and the SAP Incentive Management backend database. In my proof of concept, the Web Action performs four key responsibilities:

  • Read the incoming JSON payload.
  • Extract the SQL query and optional bind values.
  • Validate that the query is read-only.
  • Execute the query through the configured backend datasource and return JSON.

Below is a shortened version of the Web Action logic. I intentionally removed some defensive parsing branches and detailed logging to keep the blog focused.

import groovy.json.JsonOutput
import groovy.json.JsonSlurper
import groovy.transform.Field

@Field String scriptId = 'github_mcp_for_sap_im_db'

response.setContentType('application/json; charset=UTF-8')

try {
    Map payload = readPayload()
    String sqlQuery = extractSql(payload)
    List bindValues = extractBindValues(payload)

    validateReadOnlyQuery(sqlQuery)

    def db = resp.dbConnect('datasource.example')
    List<Map> rows = bindValues
        ? db.queryForList(sqlQuery, bindValues as Object[])
        : db.queryForList(sqlQuery)

    writeJson(200, [success: true, rowCount: rows.size(), rows: rows])
} catch (IllegalArgumentException ex) {
    writeJson(400, [success: false, error: ex.message])
} catch (Exception ex) {
    logger.error(scriptId, "Unexpected query execution error: ${ex.message}")
    writeJson(500, [success: false, error: 'Unable to execute SQL query.'])
}

Map readPayload() {
    String bodyText = request?.getBody()?.toString()
    if (!bodyText?.trim()) {
        throw new IllegalArgumentException('Request body is empty.')
    }

    def parsed = new JsonSlurper().parseText(bodyText)
    if (!(parsed instanceof Map)) {
        throw new IllegalArgumentException('Request body must be a JSON object.')
    }
    return parsed
}

String extractSql(Map payload) {
    for (String key : ['query', 'sql', 'sqlQuery', 'statement', 'sqlText']) {
        if (payload[key]?.toString()?.trim()) {
            return payload[key].toString().trim()
        }
    }
    throw new IllegalArgumentException('Missing SQL query.')
}

List extractBindValues(Map payload) {
    def values = payload.params ?: payload.values ?: payload.bindValues
    if (values == null) return []
    if (values instanceof List) return values
    if (values instanceof Map) return values.values().toList()
    return [values]
}

void validateReadOnlyQuery(String sqlQuery) {
    String normalized = sqlQuery.trim()
        .replaceAll(/\s+/, ' ')
        .toLowerCase(Locale.ROOT)

    if (normalized.contains(';')) {
        throw new IllegalArgumentException('Only one SQL statement is allowed.')
    }

    if (!(normalized.startsWith('select ') || normalized.startsWith('with '))) {
        throw new IllegalArgumentException('Only SELECT or WITH statements are allowed.')
    }
}

void writeJson(int statusCode, Map payload) {
    response.setStatus(statusCode)
    response.writer.write(JsonOutput.toJson(payload))
}

Why I Sanitized the Script

The datasource name, endpoint details, table names, and environment-specific configuration should not be published from a real customer or project environment. In the example above, datasource.example is only a placeholder.

The important design behavior is not the exact datasource name. The important behavior is that the Web Action script validates the request, rejects non-read operations, executes only through an approved backend connection, and returns a controlled JSON response.

Example Request Sent to Advanced Workflow

If the Web Action expects the query in a field named query, the MCP server can send a payload like this:

{
  "query": "SELECT * FROM SOME_READ_ONLY_VIEW WHERE ROWNUM <= 10"
}

If the Web Action supports bind values, the MCP server can send a payload like this:

{
  "query": "SELECT * FROM SOME_READ_ONLY_VIEW WHERE PARTICIPANT_ID = ? AND ROWNUM <= ?",
  "params": ["1001", 10]
}

Note: table names and SQL above are illustrative. Do not publish real customer table names, tenant identifiers, endpoint URLs, credentials, screenshots, or production data.

Example User Flow

  1. The user opens an MCP-compatible client, CLI, or AI assistant.
  2. The user asks: “Show me the latest transaction records for participant 1001.”
  3. The AI client decides that it needs data from SAP Incentive Management.
  4. The MCP client invokes the select_query tool on the MCP server.
  5. The MCP server validates that the query is read-only.
  6. The MCP server calls the SAP Advanced Workflow Web Action endpoint.
  7. The Web Action executes controlled backend logic and returns JSON.
  8. The MCP server returns structured data to the client.
  9. The AI agent formats the output as a table, summary, or chart.

Example MCP Tool Response Shape

The MCP server can return both a human-readable text summary and structured content:

{
  "content": [
    {
      "type": "text",
      "text": "Returned 10 rows from SAP Advanced Workflow."
    }
  ],
  "structuredContent": {
    "rows": [],
    "rowCount": 10,
    "returnedRows": 10
  }
}

This allows the AI client to reason over the data and present it in a business-friendly format.

Important Note: MCP Server as a Gateway, Not the Complete Agent

The MCP server in this proof of concept should be understood as a gateway that allows MCP-capable clients or AI CLI tools to invoke controlled backend functionality through SAP Advanced Workflow. By itself, the MCP server is not a complete business-ready AI agent.

Combining an AI CLI, an MCP client, and an MCP server can create a powerful AI-assisted interaction pattern. However, making it useful for appropriate SAP Incentive Management business scenarios would require significant design work, evaluation, and tuning. Depending on the complexity of the use case, this may require heavy prompt and tool fine-tuning, a stronger semantic layer, predefined business tools, or even a multi-agent approach where different agents handle query planning, validation, business interpretation, data analysis, and response generation.

For SAP Incentive Management scenarios, the goal should not be to let an AI agent freely explore backend data. The goal should be to expose carefully governed, business-safe capabilities that are aligned with approved support, analysis, audit, and reporting use cases.

Security and Governance Considerations

This section is the most important part of the design.

Any architecture that connects AI agents to enterprise compensation data must be treated carefully. Incentive compensation systems can contain sensitive data such as participant information, transaction values, crediting results, earnings, deposits, payment-related values, and dispute information.

1. Validate Customer and SAP Policies

Before building anything similar in a real environment, validate the customer’s policies, SAP SaaS terms, SAP support boundaries, security requirements, data privacy obligations, and enterprise architecture standards.

2. Avoid Arbitrary SQL in Production

Even if the proof of concept blocks non-read keywords, free-form SQL from an AI client is still risky. A stronger production design should use predefined tool operations and approved backend queries.

Riskier pattern:

{
  "query": "SELECT * FROM some_backend_table"
}

Safer pattern:

{
  "queryType": "participant_transactions",
  "participantId": "1001",
  "periodName": "January 2026",
  "limit": 50
}

3. Keyword Blocking Is Not Enough

The Web Action validation shown above blocks semicolons and limits statements to SELECT and WITH. That is useful for a demo, but it is not a complete SQL security model. Production implementations should consider query allowlists, database views, least-privilege datasource users, row limits, execution timeouts, bind variables, and audit logging.

4. Start Read-Only

The first version should be strictly read-only. Do not expose updates, deletes, inserts, compensation recalculation triggers, payment changes, participant updates, or plan configuration changes through an AI tool.

5. Validate Every Input

Validate query type, participant ID, period, row limit, column list, filters, and request size. Do not trust natural language input.

6. Mask Sensitive Output

Mask or remove sensitive data before returning results to the AI client. Depending on customer policy, this may include personal identifiers, payment values, email addresses, employee identifiers, bank-related fields, or other sensitive business data.

7. Enforce Authentication and Authorization

Authentication is required at every hop:

MCP Client → MCP Server
MCP Server → SAP Advanced Workflow Web Action
Advanced Workflow → Backend Data Layer

8. Add Audit Logging

Log who requested what, when, which tool was called, which query type was used, how many rows were returned, and whether the request succeeded or failed.

9. Add Rate Limiting and Timeouts

Protect the Web Action and backend database from excessive calls, large scans, accidental loops, or repeated AI retries.

What I Would Improve Next

The current proof of concept started with one read-only tool. Next improvements could include:

  • Replace free-form SQL with predefined business query tools.
  • Add a metadata endpoint that publishes allowed query types and parameters.
  • Add field-level masking rules.
  • Add a central audit log.
  • Add row-level authorization based on user context.
  • Add response pagination for larger result sets.
  • Add chart-friendly response formats for trend analysis.
  • Add a human approval step for sensitive queries.

Final Thoughts

This proof of concept shows one possible way to connect an AI agent to SAP Incentive Management data using MCP and SAP Advanced Workflow. The valuable part is not just the natural language interface. The valuable part is the controlled integration pattern:

Natural language question
        ↓
MCP tool
        ↓
Controlled Web Action
        ↓
Approved backend logic
        ↓
Structured response
        ↓
AI-assisted explanation

For support and analysis scenarios, this kind of architecture could reduce repetitive investigation effort and make data access more conversational. However, it must be designed with strict governance. Backend database access, AI agents, compensation data, and SaaS environments are not a casual combination.

This blog is only a personal technical exploration and proof of concept. Any real implementation must go through customer approval, SAP policy validation, security review, architecture review, and operational readiness assessment.

References

If the SAP Community editor does not preserve clickable links after pasting HTML, paste these references as plain URLs or use the editor's link button manually.

  1. Model Context Protocol Specification
    https://modelcontextprotocol.io/specification/2025-03-26
  2. Model Context Protocol Basic Protocol Overview
    https://modelcontextprotocol.io/specification/2025-06-18/basic
  3. SAP Help Portal: Web Action Script Example
    https://help.sap.com/docs/Advanced_Workflow/b7cb13f8b6a04157a722ad9ddfde4a28/72b77b7c7c231014a804993ce4041860.html
  4. SAP Community: Advanced Workflow White Paper - Web Actions
    https://community.sap.com/t5/human-capital-management-blog-posts-by-sap/sap-spm-incentive-management-advanced-workflow-white-paper-web-actions/ba-p/14014302
  5. SAP Help Portal: SAP Commissions
    https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_Commissions

Closing Question for the SAP Community

I would be interested to hear from the SAP Community: what are safer patterns for connecting AI agents to enterprise compensation systems, and where should the boundary be between conversational access, approved APIs, reporting tools, and backend-level investigation?

entry.title: Accelerating the Future of Commerce While Reducing Total Cost of Ownership
entry.content:
A New Chapter for SAP Commerce Cloud 

Commerce is entering a new era defined by AI assistants, agentic experiences, autonomous workflows, and composable architectures. At the same time, organizations are under pressure to reduce operating costs, simplify technology landscapes, and maximize return on existing investments. SAP Commerce Cloud is addressing both priorities simultaneously through a set of initiatives focused on reducing total cost of ownership (TCO) while creating a clear path to future innovation. 

Modernization Without Disruption 

Our strategy is simple: help customers modernize at their own pace. Rather than forcing large-scale transformations based on product deadlines, SAP is providing flexibility, support, and automation that allows organizations to align modernization efforts with business priorities, budgets, and resource availability. 

This balanced approach reduces risk while ensuring customers remain positioned to take advantage of future innovations in AI, autonomous commerce, and omnichannel experiences. 

Key Announcements Driving Customer Value 

In line with this strategy, we have recently made a couple of key announcements: 

  • Accelerator UI templates will continue to be supported without a newly announced de-release date, providing greater planning flexibility. 
  • AI-based migration tooling is being developed to simplify the transition from Accelerator-based storefronts to modern headless experiences. 
  • Investments in automated framework update tooling will reduce effort associated with Java, Spring, and frontend technology upgrades. 
  • New storefront hosting and composable architecture capabilities will increase agility and lower operational complexity. 
  • AI assistants, payment innovations, and ecosystem partnerships with Google, Vercel, Adyen, PayPal, and Checkout.com will help accelerate business outcomes. 

Headless Commerce: The Foundation for the Agentic Future 

The future of SAP Commerce Cloud is headless, composable, and API-first. Headless architecture enables organizations to deliver experiences across channels while providing the foundation required for AI assistants and autonomous agents. As commerce becomes increasingly driven by machine-to-machine interactions, APIs become essential for innovation. 

AI-Based Transformation 

A major component of SAP’s TCO reduction strategy is the introduction of AI-based migration tooling. Planned migration capabilities will analyze existing Accelerator implementations and help convert them into modern architectures, accelerating projects that historically required significant manual effort. This translates into faster modernization projects, lower implementation costs, and improved developer productivity. 

Framework modernization remains critical. Supported versions of foundational technologies help ensure security, compliance, stability, and performance. SAP is investing in automation and AI-driven update tooling to make staying current significantly easier, reducing the effort traditionally required for major framework upgrades.

Building the Foundation for Autonomous Unified Commerce 

These investments directly support SAP’s vision for Autonomous Unified Commerce. AI assistants for merchandising, shopping, and order management will connect customer experiences with operational reality. Combined with ecosystem partnerships and composable architecture options, customers gain a powerful platform ready for the next generation of digital commerce. 

What Customers and Partners Should Do Next 

Customers should assess their current architecture, plan a roadmap toward headless commerce, and prioritize required framework updates that need be done no later than August 31st, 2026. Partners play a critical role in helping organizations identify opportunities to reduce TCO, modernize incrementally, and accelerate time-to-value through best practices and implementation expertise. 

The Bottom Line 

SAP is making modernization easier, less risky, and more cost-effective. By extending flexibility around Accelerator storefronts, investing in AI-powered migration and update tooling, and building a modern composable platform for autonomous commerce, SAP Commerce Cloud is helping customers reduce total cost of ownership today while preparing for the innovations of tomorrow. 

You can learn more about this by watching the replay of the Prepare for Agentic & Autonomous Commerce and Reduce the TCO of SAP Commerce Cloud webcast. And you can find more information about the changes to the planned removal date for deprecated Accelerator UI templates and extensions here.

entry.title: What's New in SAP Business AI for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2608
entry.content:
The SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2608 release marks another important step in SAP's journey toward the Autonomous Enterprise. As SAP Business AI continues to evolve, it's no longer just about automating individual tasks. Instead, AI is becoming a trusted collaborator, handling repetitive work, surfacing the right insights, and coordinating increasingly complex business processes.

Think of a modern production line. Every station has its own responsibility, but the real efficiency comes from how smoothly everything works together. SAP Business AI plays a similar role across the enterprise, helping coordinate tasks, surface the right information at the right time, and keep business processes moving without interrupting the people who drive them.

The 2608 release brings this vision to life through innovations across multiple dimensions of enterprise AI. In this blog, we'll explore a selection of highlights spanning Agentic AI, the latest Joule innovations, and embedded AI capabilities across SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition. From intelligent finance agents and AI-powered workspaces to natural language experiences and embedded business process intelligence, these innovations are designed to make everyday work more intuitive, efficient, and context-aware.

Rather than introducing AI as a standalone capability, these innovations embed intelligence directly into the flow of work, helping finance teams accelerate period-end closing, simplifying compliance management, improving project visibility, and making interactions with SAP software feel more natural than ever before.

Take a look at the short video below to see how these AI-driven innovations are helping organizations work smarter today while bringing SAP's vision of the Autonomous Enterprise to life:

 

Agentic AI highlights

Finance

Receivables & Payables Clearing Agent (planned for 2608.2)

Matching open receivables and payables is often a repetitive and time-consuming task. Finance teams need to manually review invoices, identify matching items, and validate clearing proposals, making the process prone to delays and human error.

The new Receivables and Payables Clearing Agent uses SAP Business AI to analyze open items and generate balanced clearing proposals. The agent provides clear explanations for its matching recommendations, allowing accountants to quickly review, validate, and approve proposals before they are automatically posted as clearing documents.

By automating invoice matching and providing transparent AI recommendations, the Clearing Agent helps finance teams accelerate period-end closing, reduce manual effort, and improve operational efficiency—allowing accountants to spend less time on repetitive reconciliation tasks and more time on value-added financial analysis.

 

 

Journal entry agent (planned for 2608.3)

Creating journal entries is an essential part of every finance team's work, but preparing them from spreadsheet data often involves repetitive manual steps. Mapping fields, validating information, and creating journal entries can take valuable time, especially during busy period-end closing activities.

The new Journal Entry Agent makes this process faster and easier. Simply upload your spreadsheet through Joule, and let the agent analyze the data, map it to the required journal entry fields, and generate journal entry proposals automatically. From there, finance teams can review, adjust, and approve the proposals in the Manage Journal Entry Proposals app before posting them.

By reducing manual effort while keeping users in full control, the Journal Entry Agent helps improve accuracy, accelerate period-end closing, and free finance professionals to spend less time on routine data preparation and more time on delivering valuable financial insights.

 

 

Joule innovation highlights

Joule for Business: Moving to SAP-managed

Getting started with AI should be simple. Today, customers need to activate and configure Joule themselves by setting up the required services, identities, roles, and integrations. While this provides flexibility, it also adds complexity before users can start benefiting from SAP Business AI.

Now, Joule is transitioning from a customer-managed to a SAP-managed experience. Instead of handling the technical setup themselves, customers will simply access Joule through SAP for Me, while SAP takes care of the underlying configuration and lifecycle management. This approach also enables SAP to deliver new Joule innovations and enhancements more quickly, ensuring customers can benefit from the latest AI capabilities as they become available.

By removing setup complexity, SAP-managed Joule allows customers to focus on using AI rather than configuring it. For existing customers, a lightweight migration path is planned, with additional details and timelines to be shared in the future. Customers currently implementing Joule can continue with the existing customer-managed setup and seamlessly migrate when SAP-managed Joule becomes available.

 

 

 

Joule for Consultants

Expert Workspace

As organizations increasingly rely on AI for different business scenarios, keeping AI assistants organized and easy to manage becomes just as important as creating them. Without a structured workspace, it can be difficult to find the right AI expert or maintain specialized knowledge across projects and teams.

Expert Workspace in Joule introduces a more streamlined way to organize and manage AI Experts. Each Expert can be tailored with its own instructions and reference files, enabling more focused, context-aware conversations for specific business scenarios. New search, tagging, and filtering capabilities make it easy to quickly find the right Expert, while improved visibility into attachment storage helps users manage their growing AI workspaces more effectively.

By making AI Experts easier to organize, discover, and reuse, Expert Workspace helps users build specialized AI assistants for different workflows, improving productivity while creating a more scalable and efficient AI experience across the enterprise. 

 

 

Joule Work

As AI becomes part of everyday business, users need more than a chatbot, they need an intelligent workspace that brings together insights, automates routine work, and helps them take action. Switching between applications and manually coordinating tasks slows decision-making and reduces productivity.

With the release 2608, Joule Work introduces a dynamic AI workspace that goes beyond chat. Powered by user intent and business context, Joule Work surfaces the right insights, coordinates AI agents and assistants, and helps users complete business tasks from a single place. Whether asking questions in natural language, working in AI-generated Spaces, or accessing AI-powered workflows across desktop and mobile, users can move seamlessly from insight to action.

By bringing conversations, AI-powered workspaces, and intelligent automation together in one experience, Joule Work helps users make faster decisions, reduce context switching, and focus on the work that drives business value: another important step toward SAP's vision of the Autonomous Enterprise.

 

 

Embedded AI highlights

User Experience

AI-assisted easy fill of SAP Fiori elements-based applications

One of the highlights of this release is the general availability of AI-assisted easy fill.

Business users often receive information through emails, meeting notes, chat messages, or phone conversations. Traditionally, they then have to transfer this information manually into ERP transactions—finding the correct fields, switching between screens, and ensuring every value is entered correctly. AI-assisted easy fill removes much of this manual effort.

By simply describing a business update in natural language, SAP Business AI automatically interprets the information and populates the appropriate fields within the business object. Users can then review the suggested values before confirming them, ensuring both speed and accuracy.

This not only reduces repetitive data entry, but also helps improve data quality, lowers the learning curve for new users, and allows experienced users to complete everyday business processes significantly faster.

 

AI-assisted easy filter

We're also expanding natural language capabilities with AI-assisted easy filter. Filtering business data has traditionally required users to know exactly which filter fields exist and how they should be combined. Now, instead of manually building complex filter criteria, users can simply describe what they're looking for in plain language.

SAP Business AI understands the request and automatically translates it into the appropriate filters, including complex combinations such as amounts, currencies, dates, sorting, and grouping. Recent queries and favorite searches are also available, making frequently used searches even faster.

The result is a more intuitive search experience that helps both occasional and experienced users find business information with less effort and greater confidence.

 

Environment, Health, and Safety

AI-assisted compliance management

Managing compliance obligations often means reviewing lengthy regulations, permits, or corporate policies and manually translating them into operational tasks. It's a time-consuming process that can increase the risk of errors or overlooked obligations.

With the 2608 release, AI-assisted compliance management helps automate this work. Users can upload compliance documents as PDFs, and SAP Business AI automatically identifies relevant requirements, proposes structured compliance obligations, and recommends the corresponding operational tasks—such as periodic inspections, maintenance notifications, or reporting activities. A side-by-side view of the original document and the AI-generated suggestions allows users to quickly validate every recommendation while remaining in full control.

By reducing manual data entry and accelerating the setup of compliance obligations, AI-assisted compliance management helps organizations improve accuracy, reduce compliance risks, and stay audit-ready. Instead of spending time extracting information from lengthy documents, compliance professionals can focus on managing risks and ensuring regulatory compliance.

 

Professional Services

AI-assisted aging work in progress analytics and management

For project-based businesses, keeping track of aging work in progress (WIP) is essential for maintaining healthy cash flow. But identifying overdue WIP, understanding its root causes, and deciding what action to take often requires time-consuming analysis across multiple reports.

AI-assisted aging work in progress analytics and management helps project financial controllers gain these insights faster. Using Joule, users can analyze and visualize WIP aging across defined time buckets, while AI automatically generates executive summaries that highlight critical items and recommend the next steps. Intelligent drill-down navigation and automated billing notifications make it easier to investigate issues and accelerate resolution.

By transforming complex project data into actionable insights, this innovation helps finance and project teams reduce manual analysis, speed up billing cycles, lower cash flow risks, and spend more time making informed decisions instead of creating reports.

 

 

Summary 

🎦Register for our SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2608 Early Release Series! 

Between July 13-17, we are hosting a series of 24 expert-led live sessions to highlight the exciting innovations shipped with the SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2608 release. On our landing page you can register for the entire series or pick the sessions most relevant to you. 

 

🔎Find all our assets  

The release 2608 showcases more than 130 innovation highlights across the portfolio. Check out our release document for all the details!

We at Cloud ERP and SCM Product Success offer a service as versatile as our product itself. Check out the numerous offerings our team has created for you below:

entry.title: The AI Skills Library Is Live: Discover, Share, and Build AI Skills Together
entry.content:
The AI Skills Library is SAP's public hub for reusable AI skills, plugins, MCP servers, and marketplaces. Explore existing solutions, share your own innovations, and collaborate with developers and business users to shape the future of enterprise AI.

The AI Skills Library Is Live - Help Shape What's Next

Whether you're a developer automating technical workflows or a business user streamlining everyday tasks, you can learn from the work of others and share your own expertise with the community.

That's the idea behind SAP's AI Skills Library: an open and growing collection of reusable AI skills, plugins, MCP servers, and marketplaces. Explore what's already available, contribute your own work, and help shape the ecosystem as it evolves.

What Is a Skill?

A skill captures a workflow once, so AI assistants such as Joule or Claude can execute it consistently. Instead of repeatedly explaining how a task should be done, you define the process once and reuse it whenever needed. Skills make recurring workflows reusable, helping AI assistants perform common tasks more consistently and efficiently.

What Is the AI Skills Library?

The AI Skills Library (skills.cloud.sap) is a public directory where you can:

  • Discover reusable AI skills, MCP servers, plugins, or marketplaces
  • Install skills into supported AI environments
  • Share your own skills, plugins, or marketplaces with others
  • Learn from how other developers and business users solve recurring problems

Contributions are managed through a public GitHub repository, making openness and transparency a core part of the platform.

All published skills are clearly labeled:

  • SAP-Certified Skills – reviewed against defined quality standards
  • Community Skills – created and shared by developers and business users
  • Both contribute to building a diverse and growing ecosystem.

Getting Started

The AI Skills Library first launched at SAP Sapphire and continues to evolve with every new contribution.

Depending on your environment, skills can be installed in different ways. Installation instructions and technical documentation are available in the documentation. Alternatively, check out the AI Skills Library Tutorial.

What Can You Use Skills For?

Skills and plugins can support a wide range of repeatable tasks, from coding and content creation to meeting preparation and reporting.

Whether you're reviewing code, summarizing meetings, preparing customer conversations, or automating routine business processes, skills help AI assistants reuse workflows instead of recreating them every time.

If you find yourself repeating the same process regularly, it may be a good candidate for a skill. And if you've built something useful for yourself, there's a good chance it will help someone else too.

Help Grow the Community

The AI Skills Library is still growing, and that's intentional.

Rather than waiting until every possible use case exists, we're opening the platform early so developers, partners, customers, and business users can help shape it together.

You can already:

  • Explore existing skills
  • Publish your own skills or plugins
  • Contribute through GitHub
  • Exchange ideas and ask questions on SAP Community

Every contribution helps expand the ecosystem and gives others a starting point for their own AI workflows.

The platform is open. The repository is public. Now it's your turn - what skill will you create first?

entry.title: Getting Started with SAP Snowflake in Business Data Cloud: Build Your First ML Model
entry.content:
With the recently announced partnership, Snowflake is now available as a Solution Extension inside SAP Business Data Cloud (BDC). Together with existing offerings such as SAP HANA Cloud ML capabilities (PAL/APL), SAP Databricks, and the RPT-1.5 model, it represents another major building block in SAP's growing AI, machine learning, and data science ecosystem. This unlocks a powerful combination: your trusted SAP business data on one side, and Snowflake's AI Data Cloud with its rich ML and Cortex AI capabilities on the other - all governed, integrated, and without the traditional ETL headaches.

In this hands-on getting-started guide, we'll walk through your very first end-to-end ML project on Snowflake through BDC. We'll:

  1. Access Snowflake through SAP BDC
  2. Manually upload the classic Iris dataset
  3. Create a Snowflake Notebook and explore its Markdown, SQL, and Python cell types
  4. Use the Cortex AI Copilot to generate a new cell for us
  5. Train a simple Random Forest classifier using snowflake-ml-python
  6. Visualize the results - all without leaving the notebook

Before we start, I'd like to thank @Tobias_Mueller and @AndreasForster for their support with this blog. Now, let's dive in.

Table of contents

  • The Use Case
  • Architecture
  • Prerequisites
  • Step 1 - Access Snowflake from SAP Business Data Cloud
  • Step 2 - Set Up Warehouse, Database and Schema
  • Step 3 - Upload the Iris CSV Manually
  • Step 4 - Create a Snowflake Notebook
  • Step 5 - Explore the Three Cell Types
  • Step 6 - Generate a Cell Using Cortex AI Copilot
  • Step 7 - Verify Packages & Train a Random Forest with Snowflake ML
  • Step 8 - Show the Results (Right Inside the Notebook)
  • Step 9 - What Else You Should Know
  • Conclusion & Next Steps

The Use Case

For this getting-started guide, we'll use the classic Iris flower dataset - a small, clean dataset of 150 flower samples, each described by four measurements (sepal length, sepal width, petal length, and petal width) and labeled with one of three species: setosa, versicolor, or virginica. Our goal is a simple multi-class classification problem: predict the species of a flower from its measurements.

Why Iris? It's the "Hello World" of machine learning. Small enough to train in seconds, yet rich enough to demonstrate a complete end-to-end ML workflow: data ingestion, exploration, model training, and evaluation. This lets us focus on how the SAP BDC × Snowflake platform works rather than on data wrangling.

But think of Iris as a stand-in for your real SAP business data. The exact same pattern - take structured records, engineer features, train a classifier, and score new data natively in Snowflake - maps directly onto high-value enterprise scenarios, for example:

      • Customer churn prediction on CRM / Sales Cloud data
      • Demand forecasting on S/4HANA sales and inventory data
      • Employee attrition modeling on SuccessFactors data
      • Invoice or expense anomaly detection on Ariba / finance data

In each case, the four "petal and sepal measurements" simply become your business features (e.g., contract length, order frequency, tenure), and the "species" becomes your business outcome (e.g., churn / no-churn). Once you've completed this walkthrough with Iris, you'll have the blueprint to swap in genuine SAP data products and derive real insights - without moving data out of your governed landscape.

Architecture

Before we get hands-on, let's understand where Snowflake fits within the SAP Business Data Cloud landscape.

The Big Picture: Snowflake as a BDC Solution Extension

At the highest level, SAP Business Data Cloud unifies your SAP data sources (S/4HANA, HCM, SuccessFactors, Concur, and more) into a governed Business Data Fabric. SAP Snowflake plugs in as a Solution Extension, bringing Zero-Copy data access and Custom AI/ML capabilities right alongside SAP BW PCE, the BDC Cockpit, and SAP Datasphere - all consuming from a shared object store of SAP and custom data products.

The End-to-End Flow: From SAP Data to ML Insights

In a full production scenario, the journey of data looks like this: SAP data products are defined at the source (e.g., S/4HANA PCE), exposed through the SAP Foundation Service, and shared into the BDC Cockpit / SAP Datasphere Data Catalog. From there, they flow zero-copy into the SAP Snowflake Unity/Horizon Catalog, where a Notebook trains a model and produces an ML Dataset. That dataset can then be shared back to SAP Datasphere and consumed by analytic models, views, or a story in SAP Analytics Cloud - closing the loop from raw SAP data to actionable ML insight.

 

Our Focus for This Guide

That end-to-end flow is powerful, but it involves several SAP components working together. For this getting-started guide, we'll keep things simple and stay entirely within SAP Snowflake. Uploading data manually and training a model in a notebook so we can focus purely on learning the tool itself. Once you're comfortable here, the zero-copy integration with the rest of BDC is the natural next step.

 

Prerequisites

      • Access to SAP Business Data Cloud with the Snowflake Solution Extension enabled
      • A Snowflake account/warehouse provisioned through BDC
      • Basic knowledge of SQL and Python
      • The Iris dataset CSV (download from this GitHub gist)

Step 1 - Access Snowflake from SAP Business Data Cloud

From your SAP BDC launchpad, navigate to the Snowflake Solution Extension tile. This opens Snowflake's Snowsight UI, already authenticated with your SAP identity. No separate login, no context switching.

 

💡The Snowflake environment provisioned through BDC is a fully managed Snowflake account, but with governance and identity tied back to your SAP landscape.

Step 2 - Set Up Warehouse, Database and Schema

Instead of using SQL commands, we'll create our Snowflake objects through the intuitive Snowsight UI.

Create a Warehouse

      1. In Snowsight, navigate to Home.
      2. Click + Create Warehouse.
      3. Configure your warehouse:
        • Name: ML_DEMO_WH
        • Size: X-Small (recommended for this demo)
        • Auto Suspend: 60 minutes
        • Auto Resume: Enabled (default)
      4. Click Create Warehouse.

Create a Database and Schema

      1. Navigate to Catalog → Database Explorer (or Data → Databases).
      2. Click + Database and enter the name ML_DEMO_DB, then click Create.
      3. Open your new ML_DEMO_DB database, click + Schema, enter the name IRIS, and click Create.

 

Step 3 - Upload the Iris CSV Manually

Now let's get our training data into Snowflake. No scripts, just clicks. There are two ways to reach the data loading wizard:

      • Option A: From Home, click + Upload local files.
      • Option B: Navigate to Ingestion → Add Data → Load data into a table.

Either path opens the same load-data wizard. From there:

      1. Select the ML_DEMO_WH warehouse on the top right of the pop-up window
      2. Choose the ML_DEMO_DB.IRIS schema and enter a new table name: IRIS_DATA.
      3. Drag and drop your iris.csv file into the wizard.
      4. Snowflake will auto-detect the schema. Confirm the column types (four NUMBER features and one VARCHAR species column).
      5. Click Load.

 

You can verify the upload by clicking on View table details. Here you'll see a preview of your data, can check out the Data Quality or Table Details and explore the Lineage of the IRIS_DATA table.

 

Step 4 - Create a Snowflake Notebook

Navigate to Projects → Workspaces (My Workspace) → + Add new → Notebook.

      • Name: iris_ml_demo
      • Location: ML_DEMO_DB.IRIS
      • Connected Service: Use a Service with Python 3.12 v2.3, a compute pool with at least CPU_X64_S (ideal for hosting multiple services/jobs while saving cost) & Artifact SNOWPARK
      • Warehouse: ML_DEMO_WH

 

Step 5 - Explore the Three Cell Types

Snowflake Notebooks support three different cell types. You can add the one you need by clicking the corresponding button at the bottom of the notebook, or by inserting a new cell and changing its type using the dropdown in the top-left corner. Let's explore all three.

Markdown Cell

# Iris Classification with Snowflake ML
This notebook trains a Random Forest classifier on the Iris dataset,
using data stored natively in Snowflake and the `snowflake-ml-python` library.

SQL Cell

Name this cell & its output iris_sql (the name matters. We'll reference it from Python):

SELECT * FROM ML_DEMO_DB.IRIS.IRIS_DATA;

Add a second SQL cell for a quick sanity check:

SELECT species, 
       AVG(sepal_length) AS avg_sepal_length,
       AVG(petal_length) AS avg_petal_length
FROM ML_DEMO_DB.IRIS.IRIS_DATA
GROUP BY species;

Python Cell

Here's the magic. You can reference the SQL cell's result directly:

# Reference the SQL cell's result directly and make a working copy
df = iris_sql.copy()
print(df.shape)
df.head()

 

Step 6 - Generate a Cell Using Cortex AI Copilot

Snowflake Notebooks come with a built-in Cortex AI Copilot. Click the ✨ (Ask Copilot) icon in the sidebar to open the chat panel.

Try a prompt like:

"Generate a Python cell that plots the distribution of each numeric feature grouped by species using matplotlib."

Cortex will produce something like:

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

numeric_cols = ['SEPAL_LENGTH', 'SEPAL_WIDTH', 'PETAL_LENGTH', 'PETAL_WIDTH']
species_list = df['SPECIES'].unique()

fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 2, figsize=(12, 8))
axes = axes.flatten()

for i, col in enumerate(numeric_cols):
    for species in species_list:
        subset = df[df['SPECIES'] == species]
        axes[i].hist(subset[col], alpha=0.6, label=species, bins=15)
    axes[i].set_title(f'Distribution of {col}')
    axes[i].set_xlabel(col)
    axes[i].set_ylabel('Frequency')
    axes[i].legend()

plt.suptitle('Feature Distributions by Species', fontsize=14)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Click Keep all so the newly generated python cell is inserted in the Notebook. No copy-paste needed.

 

💡Try other prompts like "Split the data into train and test sets" or "Show me a correlation heatmap of the features".

Fix an Error with Cortex

Copilot doesn't just generate code - it can also fix it. To demonstrate this, we'll intentionally introduce a typo in the following Python cell. The misspelled describe() method (descibe) will trigger an AttributeError, allowing us to see how Cortex identifies and fixes the issue.

# Generate a statistical summary of the numeric features
numeric_cols = ['SEPAL_LENGTH', 'SEPAL_WIDTH', 'PETAL_LENGTH', 'PETAL_WIDTH']
summary = df[numeric_cols].descibe()
print(summary)

Run the cell to trigger the intentional AttributeError. Rather than debugging it manually, click the ✨Fix error button that appears alongside the error. Cortex reads the traceback, pinpoints the problem, and suggests the corrected code which you can apply with a single click.

 

Step 7 - Verify Packages & Train a Random Forest with Snowflake ML

You may have noticed we never had to install any packages. That's because Snowflake Notebooks come with ~100 of the most popular data-science and ML packages preinstalled, along with 1st-party Snowflake packages such as snowflake-snowpark-python.

Before we dive into training, let's quickly confirm the packages we need are available. This is just a check, not an import:

from importlib.metadata import version, PackageNotFoundError

for pkg in ["snowflake-ml-python", "pandas", "scikit-learn", "matplotlib"]:
    try:
        print(f"✅ {pkg}: {version(pkg)}")
    except PackageNotFoundError:
        print(f"❌ {pkg}: NOT available")

Now the fun part. The snowflake.ml.modeling library brings a scikit-learn–like API directly onto Snowflake, so training runs in Snowflake.

from snowflake.snowpark.context import get_active_session
from snowflake.ml.modeling.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier

session = get_active_session()
session.sql("USE DATABASE ML_DEMO_DB").collect()
session.sql("USE SCHEMA IRIS").collect()

# Load data as a Snowpark DataFrame
snow_df = session.table("IRIS_DATA")

# Split into train and test
train_df, test_df = snow_df.random_split(weights=[0.8, 0.2], seed=42)

# Define feature columns and target
FEATURE_COLS = ["SEPAL_LENGTH", "SEPAL_WIDTH", "PETAL_LENGTH", "PETAL_WIDTH"]
LABEL_COL = "SPECIES"
OUTPUT_COL = "PREDICTED_SPECIES"

# Train the model
model = RandomForestClassifier(
    input_cols=FEATURE_COLS,
    label_cols=LABEL_COL,
    output_cols=OUTPUT_COL,
    n_estimators=100,
    random_state=42,
)
model.fit(train_df)

# Predict on the test set
predictions = model.predict(test_df)
predictions.select(FEATURE_COLS + [LABEL_COL, OUTPUT_COL]).show(10)

When you run this cell, Snowflake spins up the training in-database. After fit() completes, predict() appends a PREDICTED_SPECIES column, and the last line prints a 10-row sample so you can eyeball the model's predictions against the actual species.

Step 8 - Show the Results (Right Inside the Notebook)

Let's evaluate our model with three quick views:

from sklearn.metrics import accuracy_score, confusion_matrix
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

pred_pd = predictions.to_pandas()

# 1. Accuracy
acc = accuracy_score(pred_pd[LABEL_COL], pred_pd[OUTPUT_COL])
print(f"✅ Test accuracy: {acc:.3f}")

# 2. Confusion matrix
cm = pd.crosstab(pred_pd[LABEL_COL], pred_pd[OUTPUT_COL],
                 rownames=['Actual'], colnames=['Predicted'])
print("\nConfusion matrix:")
print(cm)

# 3. Feature importances
sk_model = model.to_sklearn()
importances = pd.Series(sk_model.feature_importances_, index=FEATURE_COLS)\
                .sort_values()

importances.plot(kind='barh', title='Feature Importances', figsize=(6, 3))
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

You'll typically see an accuracy around 0.93–1.00 on Iris, with petal length and petal width as the top features.

 

That's it! A full ML workflow, entirely inside Snowflake, launched from SAP Business Data Cloud.

Step 9 - What Else You Should Know

Before you go, here are two handy features tucked away in the notebook's Tools Panel at the very bottom of the screen. They are completely independent from each other.

Explore Cell Dependencies in the Tools Panel

Open the Tools Panel at the very bottom of the notebook and switch to the Dependencies tab. Here Snowflake visualizes how the cells throughout your notebook are linked with each other. For example, how our Python cell references the iris_sql result, and how later cells build on that DataFrame and the trained model. This dependency view helps you:

  • Understand the flow of data from one cell to the next at a glance.
  • See which cells depend on which, so you know exactly what needs to re-run when you change something upstream.
  • Trace and debug your notebook logic without manually scanning through every cell.

Schedule Notebooks with the Job Scheduler

The Job Scheduler is another capability in the notebook. With it, you can turn your notebook into a recurring job - set a schedule (e.g., hourly or daily), pick a warehouse, and let Snowflake run it automatically. This is perfect for repeatable training or reporting, with no external orchestrator needed.

 

Conclusion & Next Steps

In under 30 minutes, you've gone from "I have SAP BDC access" to "I have a trained ML model running natively in Snowflake." That's the power of the SAP × Snowflake partnership: less plumbing, more insights. While this tutorial focused on building and running ML directly in the SAP Snowflake environment, a production solution would typically continue by writing predictions or enriched datasets back to SAP Business Data Cloud, where they can be consumed across SAP applications.

Where to go next:

      • SAP Business Data Cloud, Snowflake edition
      • Zero-Copy integration between SAP and Snowflake
      • Snowflake ML documentation
      • Snowflake Cortex AI documentation

What's coming next in this series

This is just the beginning. I'm planning a series of follow-up blogs exploring more of what SAP Snowflake has to offer. Here's what's coming soon:

      • Building Streamlit Apps in Snowflake - using the built-in Streamlit functionality to create interactive data apps directly inside Snowflake, no separate hosting required.
      • From SAP-Managed to Custom-Managed Data Products - consuming SAP-managed Data Products, building your own custom-managed Data Products in SAP Snowflake, and sharing them back into the SAP landscape.
      • Bringing SAP Datasphere / HANA Cloud ML into SAP Snowflake - leveraging the ML capabilities of SAP Datasphere / HANA Cloud from within SAP Snowflake by allowing External Access Integration.

Connect with me on LinkedIn to stay up to date and get notified when the next blogs in this series go live.

Now it's your turn. What SAP data would you love to run an ML model on through Snowflake? Drop your ideas in the comments below.

Happy modeling! 🦾

entry.title: From Code to Strategy: How MCP, CPI, and Lightweight Agents Unlock AI‑Driven SAP Integrations
entry.content:
Introduction

SAP Cloud Platform Integration (CPI) allows enterprises to connect diverse systems through iFlows — integration flows that define how data moves and transforms between endpoints. By combining CPI with the Model Context Protocol (MCP) and a lightweight Agent, we can expose SAP processes as consumable services — enabling seamless orchestration, automation, and intelligent interaction across enterprise systems.

 

Understanding the Components

SAP Cloud Platform Integration (CPI)

SAP CPI is the integration backbone of the SAP ecosystem. It enables secure, scalable, and real-time data exchange between SAP and non-SAP systems. In this architecture, CPI acts as the execution layer, where business logic resides. The iFlow processes requests coming from the MCP Server, performs necessary transformations, and interacts with SAP backend systems such as S/4HANA, SuccessFactors, or Finance modules.

In the AI world: CPI provides the structured data foundation that intelligent agents rely on. By exposing iFlows as APIs, it allows AI systems to access enterprise data securely and contextually — bridging traditional ERP with modern AI-driven automation.

 

Model Context Protocol (MCP) Server

The MCP Server is the middleware bridge between the Agent and SAP CPI. It defines “tools” — essentially API endpoints — that the Agent can call to trigger specific SAP processes. It handles authentication, routing, and error management, ensuring that requests from the Agent are properly formatted and securely transmitted to CPI.

In the AI world: MCP acts as the contextual translator between natural language and enterprise APIs. It enables AI agents to understand what business functions are available and how to invoke them, making enterprise systems conversational and intelligent.

 

Copilot Agent
