The Agent is the frontline interface — a lightweight application (built with FastAPI or similar frameworks) that interacts with users or other systems. It consumes the MCP tools and provides a simple way to trigger SAP processes, either through a web UI or API calls.

In the AI world: The Agent represents the intelligence layer — capable of interpreting user intent, orchestrating workflows, and connecting to enterprise data through MCP and CPI. It’s the component that transforms static APIs into dynamic, AI-driven interactions.

 

Key Steps

1. Expose the iFlow in SAP CPI

  • Design and deploy your iFlow (e.g., invoice retrieval or employee data).

  • Ensure it’s accessible via HTTPS with proper authentication.

2. Create the MCP Server

  • Define tools mapping to CPI endpoints.

  • Configure the MCP server to expose these tools securely.

  • Example endpoint: http://localhost:8000/tools/sayHello

3. Consume with an Agent

  • Build a lightweight Agent using FastAPI.

  • Connect it to the MCP server and expose an endpoint like http://localhost:9000/agent/sayHello.

  • Provide a simple UI for users to trigger CPI processes.

 

Tools Used

  • SAP CPI → iFlow design and deployment

  • MCP Server → Tool definition and orchestration

  • FastAPI → Agent development

  • OAuth2 / SAP IAS → Authentication and security

 

Architecture Overview

Embed your architecture diagram here showing the flow: User → Copilot Agent → MCP Server → SAP CPI → SAP Backend Systems

Figure 1: End-to-end architecture connecting the Agent, MCP Server, and SAP CPI iFlow.

Step: Create a Simple iFlow in SAP CPI

Design a basic iFlow in SAP Cloud Platform Integration (CPI) that accepts a name as input payload and returns a greeting message. For example, if the payload contains "Dibyajyoti", the iFlow should respond with "Hello Dibyajyoti".

Once the development is complete, deploy the iFlow to generate the endpoint URL. This URL will be used later by the MCP Server and Copilot Agent to invoke the iFlow and integrate it into the overall architecture.

"https://<SUBACCOUNT>.it-cpitrial06-rt.cfapps.us10-001.hana.ondemand.com/http/hello"

Step 2: Test the iFlow Endpoint in Postman

Once the iFlow is deployed in SAP CPI, you’ll receive an endpoint URL. To validate it, use Postman:

  1. Open Postman and create a new request.

  2. Set the method to POST (or GET, depending on your iFlow design).

  3. Enter the endpoint URL generated during deployment (e.g., https://cpi.example.com/http/endpoint/sayHello).

  4. Authentication:

    • Username → Client ID

    • Password → Client Secret These credentials are provided when you configure the CPI tenant and are required for secure access.

  5. Payload:

    • In the request body, pass the input JSON or text payload (e.g., { "name": "Dibyajyoti" }).

  6. Send the request and verify the response.

    • Expected output: Hello Dibyajyoti

 

Step 3: Set Up the MCP Environment

Before working with the MCP Server, ensure your development environment is ready. You’ll need the following installed on your system:

  • Python (version 3.9 or above recommended)

  • PIP (Python package manager)

  • Visual Studio Code (for editing and running your project)


Step 4: Create the Project Folder Structure

To organize your MCP setup, create the following folders:

  1. Main Project Folder

    • Name: MCPPROJECT

  2. Subfolders inside MCPPROJECT

    • SAP_MCP_SERVER → Contains the MCP Server files (server.py, .env, requirements.txt)

    • COPILOT_AGENT → Contains the Agent files (agent.py, templates/index.html, requirements.txt)

 

SAP_MCP_SERVER

.env---->

requirements.txt---->

server.py---->

COPILOT_AGENT

requirements.txt------>

agent.py------->

create a subfolder

index.html--------->

 

Step 5: Run the MCP Server and Copilot Agent

Inside sap_mcp_server

Install dependencies and start the MCP Server:

pip install -r requirements.txt
uvicorn server:app --reload --port 8000

 

  • pip install -r requirements.txt → Installs all required Python packages.

  • uvicorn server:app --reload --port 8000 → Launches the MCP Server on port 8000 with auto-reload enabled.

Inside copilot_agent

Install dependencies and start the Copilot Agent:

pip install -r requirements.txt
uvicorn agent:app --reload --port 9000
​

 

  • pip install -r requirements.txt → Installs Agent dependencies.

  • uvicorn agent:app --reload --port 9000 → Launches the Agent on port 9000 with auto-reload enabled.

Test the Agent

Once both services are running, open the Agent URL in your browser:

http://localhost:9000/

 This will load the Agent’s web interface, allowing you to trigger the MCP tools and ultimately invoke the SAP CPI iFlow.

 

Conclusion

By exposing SAP CPI iFlows through an MCP Server and consuming them with an Agent, enterprises can create a modular, API-driven integration layer. This approach simplifies access to SAP processes, improves reusability, and lays the foundation for AI-powered enterprise automation — where intelligent agents can interact with SAP systems naturally, securely, and efficiently.

Regards,

Dibyajyoti Nanda

https://www.linkedin.com/in/dibyajyoti-nanda-23907429/

entry.title: Solving the Last Mile of Invoice-to-PO Line Matching in SAP MM & FI. Does traditional ML help here?
entry.content:
The Problem: A Month-End Nightmare

While working with an accounts payable team, I watched month-end turn into a nightmare in a PO-based MIRO world. Every open supplier invoice has to post before the books close, and leadership watches blocked and unmatched invoices like a Canis-Lupus.

So as the SAP specialist on the ground, I dug in. Turns out a big chunk of those stuck invoices had nothing to do with price or qty variance. They simply had no safe way to match invoice lines to PO line items  the invoice didn't mirror the PO's structure, the vendor never referenced PO line numbers, and SAP had nothing to propose a clean match from.

So it fell to a person. Invoice open on one screen, MIRO on the other, manually mapping every line to the right PO or GR before the system would even let it through. Multiply that by a stack of invoices at month-end, and a "standard" process turns into a grind nobody signed up for.

ChatGPT-Prompt: Can you please generate image for The Problem: A Month-End Nightmare in SAP MIRO, PO Invoice 2 PO Matching.

The Invoice Already Knows the PO. Just Not the Line.

Here's the thing nobody outside AP really understands about this problem: the invoice does tell you which PO it belongs to. UBL 2.1 and Peppol BIS 3.0 e-invoices carry the PO reference right there in the header 

<cac:OrderReference>PO-4500067890</cac:OrderReference> 
(OR)
<cbc:BuyerReference>PO-4500067890</cbc:BuyerReference>

So SAP knows the invoice is against PO-4500012345, PO-4500067890, and PO-4500099001.

What it doesn't know is which of the dozens of lines across those three POs each invoice line actually maps to. That mapping only exists at the header level. Line-level PO references are technically possible in the standard  I've seen maybe one vendor in the wild actually populate them. Everyone else leaves you to figure it out.

And "figure it out" usually means a human, squinting at two descriptions:

Invoice says

PO says

Hex Bolts M10

Hex Head Bolt M10x50mm Grade 8.8

Office Paper A4 500 sheets

A4 Copy Paper Ream 500

Same item. Zero words in common that matter. A junior AP clerk gets this right in about two seconds. A rules engine built on exact-match or Levenshtein distance gets it wrong every time, because the problem was never spelling  it's meaning.

  • Live demo: https://karthionlupenix-invmatchmaker.hf.space

Why Not Just Call an LLM?

The obvious answer today is "just call an LLM." It would probably match "Hex Bolts M10" to "Hex Head Bolt M10x50mm Grade 8.8" just fine if you asked it nicely. But an LLM call per invoice line, multiplied by every candidate PO line, across every invoice a company processes in a month, is slow and expensive for what is fundamentally a narrow, repetitive, high-volume matching task  not a reasoning task. You don't need something that can write you a poem to know two part descriptions refer to the same bolt.

Traditional machine learning doesn't get the hype right now. But for "does this invoice line mean the same thing as this PO line," it's the right tool, not the consolation prize.

The Practical Approach

The knowledge I picked up from University of Auckland's MAI course and Stanford's Machine Learning course pointed me toward a Multi-Layer Perceptron approach. This isn't a full AP automation suite there are enough of those already, and most are still doing header-level three-way match dressed up as "AI." I built specifically the part that's missing: given an invoice and the POs it already references, which PO line does each invoice line belong to.

Technical deep dive (skip ahead to "Why This Instead of Buying Off the Shelf" if you're not into the Machine Learning details)

Sentence-BERT (all-MiniLM-L6-v2, pre-trained, no fine-tuning yet) encodes both descriptions into 384-dimension vectors and takes the cosine similarity. That alone gets you most of the way "Hex Bolts M10" and "Hex Head Bolt M10x50mm Grade 8.8" land close together in embedding space even though they share almost no substring.

But semantic similarity alone isn't enough to post against, so I layer eight features on top of it per invoice/PO-line pair:

Feature

Description

Weight

F0

semantic_similarity

0.30

F1

quantity_ratio

0.15

F2

unit_price_ratio

0.12

F3

uom_match

0.08

F4

net_value_ratio

0.10

F5

material_number_match

0.10

F6

material_group_match

0.07

F7

vendor_material_match

0.08

Weighted sum, blended 40/60 with the raw semantic score, clipped to [0,1], sigmoid. Top-k (k=3) candidates per invoice line, greedy conflict resolution so two invoice lines don't both claim the same PO line, then triage:

  • ≥ 90% → auto-suggested, safe to post without a human touching it
  • 60–89% → goes to a reviewer, with the top-3 candidates shown so they're picking, not typing
  • < 60% → manual resolution  the AI is telling you honestly that it doesn't know

What's Still a Prototype (Being Upfront)

That weighted scorer is a stand-in. I'm calling it "LightGBM" in the docs because that's what it becomes in the next phase, but right now it's a fixed weight vector I hand-tuned against a handful of sample invoices not a model trained on labelled data. If you've read anything I've written before, you know I don't like shipping something and letting the framing overstate what it actually does. This is proof that the shape of the solution works, not a claim that the numbers above are production-grade.

The honest next step is the same one I took with the SAP ticket classifier I wrote about a few weeks back: get real labelled pairs actual accept/override/reject decisions from an AP team, or anonymized MIRO history and train an actual ranker on them instead of a vector I picked by eye. Everything else (the SAP MM field alignment, the INVOIC02 IDoc output, the triage thresholds) is close to done. The learning is the part that's still owed.

Why This Instead of Buying Something Off the Shelf

Most AP automation platforms are excellent at OCR and header-level tolerance matching, and I have no interest in rebuilding that. What they're mostly not built for is this specific line-level semantic step Rossum's own PO-matching extension, for instance, checks whether line items match by ID and field overlap, not by meaning. That's the gap this sits in: not a capture tool, not a three-way match engine, just the piece that answers "which line" once the header-level "which PO" is already known.

Related Work
This isn't the first attempt at line-level semantic matching. A 2020 paper, Online Similarity Learning with Feedback for Invoice Line Item Matching (arXiv:2001.00288), tackled the same gap using NER and taxonomy-based Jaccard similarity with a human-feedback loop, rather than embeddings. It's worth a read if you want to see how this problem was approached before Sentence-BERT-style models made semantic similarity cheap and fast.

Try It

If your team is still doing this matching by hand and you're curious what a semantic-first approach looks like on real SAP MM fields, the MVP is live invoice XML in, SAP-ready IDoc XML out, nothing hidden behind a demo request form.

Live demo and docs linked below. This is a Phase 1 proof of concept feedback on where it breaks is genuinely more useful to me than praise.

Author: If you find this information helpful, please consider clicking the "Like" button on this blog and sharing your thoughts in the comments section below. You can also connect with the author on their LinkedIn profile: [Author's LinkedIn Profile]'

Reference and Credits:

  • Machine Learning Course - @ Stanford, by Professor Andrew Ng
  • AI Fundamentals Course (COMPSCI 713) - @ University of Auckland, by Professors Thomas and Xinyu
  • ML Perceptrons: https://www.cs.auckland.ac.nz/~pat/706_98/ln/node134.html#SECTION00077000000000000000
  • Research Paper (Harnessing label semantics): https://arxiv.org/pdf/2212.01685
  • Levenshtein Distance: https://dl.acm.org/doi/10.5555/1822502
  • Cosine Similarity: https://www.ibm.com/think/topics/cosine-similarity
  • Cosine Similarity Image: https://machinelearningplus.com/wp-content/uploads/2018/10/soft-cosine.png
  • Sigmoid Activation: https://medium.com/data-science/sigmoid-activation-and-binary-crossentropy-a-less-than-perfect-match...
  • HuggingFace Follow on: https://huggingface.co/KarthionLupenix
  • Ollama Sentence-BERT: https://ollama.com/library/all-minilm:l6-v2
  • Peppol documentation: https://docs.peppol.eu/poacc/upgrade-3/profiles/63-invoiceresponse/
  • Image Generation: ChatGPT prompt - "Can you please generate image for The Problem: A Month-End Nightmare in SAP MIRO, PO Invoice 2 PO Matching."
  • Demo video link: https://youtu.be/XVqf4347mOI
  • Thanks: Former colleagues in Singapore, NZ, Germany, and India who shared their firsthand experience with the AP matching problem that started this. Thank you boys and girls. 🙂

entry.title: Digitalisierung und Skalierung von Change Management: Erkenntnisse aus der SAP Kaffee-Ecke
entry.content:
Die monatliche SAP Change und Training Kaffee-Ecke widmete sich einem hochaktuellen Thema: Wie lassen sich Change Management-Prozesse in SAP-Projekten digitalisieren und skalieren? PD Dr. Oliver Kohnke, Global Service Owner for Organizational Change Management bei SAP, gab Einblicke in die Herausforderungen, Lösungsansätze und die neue AI-Assisted User Learning and Change Management App.

Warum wird Skalierung immer wichtiger?

Die Eingangsfrage an die Teilnehmenden machte deutlich: In den meisten Organisationen ist die Digitalisierung von Change Management bereits ein wichtiges Thema. Die Hälfte der Teilnehmenden stimmten dieser Aussage voll und ganz zu.

Die zweite Frage fokussierte sich auf die Herausforderungen, die bei der Digitalisierung von Change Management bestehen und gab zentrale Hinweise, die es in der Praxis zu berücksichtigen gilt: Akzeptanz steht mit deutlichem Abstand an erster Stelle, gefolgt von Kompetenz und Widerständen. Weitere genannte Aspekte waren fehlendes Verständnis, Budget- und Kapazitätsmangel, fehlende Plattformen sowie Ängste und Verunsicherung. Der Hinweis auf "Human in the Loop" verdeutlicht: Trotz KI-Unterstützung müssen Menschen im Prozess verbleiben.

Welche Herausforderungen bestehen bei der Digitalisierung von Change Management?

Die abschließende Frage ergab, dass die praktische Erfahrung mit Change Management-Apps in der Community noch begrenzt ist – die meisten Teilnehmenden haben bisher keine Berührungspunkte mit solchen Tools gesammelt.

Was treibt die Notwendigkeit zur Digitalisierung und Skalierung von Change Management? Oliver Kohnke stellte gleich zu Beginn klar: Der technologische Wandel vollzieht sich schneller als Mitarbeitende und Organisationen ihn adaptieren können. Die zentrale Herausforderung ist dabei nicht die Technologie selbst, sondern die Adoption – also die Annahme und Nutzung neuer Technologien. Gleichzeitig ist die kontinuierliche, manuelle Anpassung von Trainings- und Change Management-Inhalten zu langsam und die Kosten zu hoch. Um diese Herausforderungen zu bewältigen, müssen Change Management und Training ebenfalls schneller und effizienter werden.

Welche Vision verfolgt SAP?

SAP möchte Organizational Change Management Services und Tools anbieten, die “fully digitized, low touch, easy to use und highly scalable” sind. Diese Skalierbarkeit ist entscheidend, da SAP weltweit eine große Anzahl von Cloud-Implementierungen begleitet. Das Ziel: Kunden und Partner sollen die Angebote einfach und schnell nutzen können.

Um skalieren zu können, ist die Digitalisierung der standardisierten Methodik und Inhalte eine wesentliche Voraussetzung.

SAP nutzt seit Jahren eine “Best Practice” Change Management Methodologie. Der SAP Change Management-Ansatz ist auf SAP’s Activate Implementierungsmethodik abgestimmt und beinhaltet sechs Dimensionen: Change Strategy, Change Leadership, Change Communication, Change Realization, Change Enablement und Change Activeness.

 

Welche digitalen Assets stellt SAP bereit?

Methodik im SAP Activate Roadmap Viewer

Die Change Management Methodik ist über den SAP Activate Roadmap Viewer kostenfrei verfügbar. Diese Kundenplattform bietet Roadmaps für verschiedene SAP-Produkte auf Basis der globalen Implementierungsmethodik.

Der Change Management-Ansatz ist dort entsprechend der Activate-Phasen mit 18 Deliverables, 72 Tasks, 50 Tools & Templates und 10 Weblinks hinterlegt. Eine Registrierung auf sap.com ist notwendig, um die Tools und Templates nutzen zu können.

Ein besonderer Vorteil: Der Roadmap Viewer hat eine direkte Verbindung zu SAP Cloud Application Lifecycle Management (CALM). Wer CALM nutzt, kann die Deliverables und Templates direkt in das Tool laden und als Change Manager damit arbeiten.

SAP Learning Journeys und Zertifizierung

Um Kunden und Partnern die Nutzung der Change Managememt Methodik zu erleichtern, bietet SAP verschiedene Learning Courses an, die in einer Learning Journey unter dem Titel "Managing the People Side of SAP Cloud Projects" zusammengefasst sind. Die Lerninhalte werden auf Basis von verschiedenen Projektszenarien praxisnah vermittelt und sind über die Plattform learning.sap.com kostenfrei verfügbar.

Abschliessen kann man die Lernpfade mit einer offiziellen SAP Zertifizierung. Die Zertifizierung erfolgt AI unterstützt: In einem interaktiven Szenario mit einem Avatar werden Prüflinge in realitätsnahen Szenarien getestet . Die Zertifizierung ist entweder über ein Abonnement des SAP Learning Hub abrufbar oder kann im Trainingsshop der SAP erworben werden.

OCM in den SAP Success Plans

Das neue Service und Support Offering der SAP, die Success Plans, beinhaltet drei aufeinander aufbauende Layers: Foundational, Advanced und Max. Der Foundational und Advanced Success Plan beinhalten bereits verschiedene Change Management und Learning Services. Für den Max Success Plan sind die Inhalte zum Zeitpunkt der Coffee Corner Session noch in Arbeit).

Alle oben beschriebenen Angebote (SAP Activate Roadmap Viewer, CALM, Learning Journeys) sind auch Teil des Foundational Success Plan, den jeder Cloud Kunde automatisch erhält.

Mit dem Advanced Success Plan bekommen Kunden darüber hinaus Zugang zu verschiedenen standardisierten Change Management- und Learning-Services und Experten. Die Angebote stehen seit Mai 2026 zur Verfügung:

Darüber hinaus sind mehrere Change Management und Learning Advisory Services für den Max Success Plan in Arbeit.

 

Was leistet die neue AI-Assisted User Learning and Change Management App?

Im Juni 2026 ging die AI-Assisted User Learning and Change Management App in die General Availability. Die Anwendung ist über den SAP Micro-App Hub verfügbar. Fokus der ersten Version sind die Lernfunktionalitäten wie AI assisted Lernbedarfsanalyse und Lerninhalterstellung. Darüber hinaus bietet die App auch erste Funktionen zur Unterstützung von Change cManagement in Projekten zur Verfügung, auf die wir uns in dieser Session konzentriert haben.

Welche Funktionen bietet die App User Learning and Change Management?

Die erste Version konzentriert sich auf vier zentrale Change-Aktivitäten:

Stakeholder-Identifikation - Der Startpunkt jedes Change-Projekts:

Auf Basis von SAP Best Practices bietet die App vordefinierte Listen mit typischen betroffenen Rollen, die kundenspezifisch anpassbar sind. Wichtige Informationen wie Anzahl der betroffenen Personen, Line of Business und regionale Verteilung lassen sich für jede Stakeholdergruppe erfassen.

Die App unterstützt derzeit SAP Best Practices für S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition und Public Edition sowie einige SuccessFactors-Module. Die Integration weiterer SAP-Lösungen ist in Arbeit.

Change Story - Das Fundament der Change-Kommunikation:

Die App strukturiert diese nach Need for Change (Current Reality, Drivers, Alternatives), Future Vision, Implementation Approach (Key Facts and Figures), Value and Impact sowie Core Messaging. Alle Informationen, die typischerweise in Entscheidungsgremien vor Projektstart erstellt und diskutiert werden, lassen sich zentral dokumentieren. Das Ziel: Keine gemischten Botschaften, sondern konsistente Kommunikation. Die Change Story kann generell und für spezifische Stakeholdergruppen erstellt werden.

Kommunikationsanalyse:

Change Manager müssen wissen, welche Kanäle verfügbar sind – von Intranet-Portalen über Employee Meetings bis zu E-Mails oder Blogs. Die App hilft bei der Identifikation und Zuordnung geeigneter Kanäle für verschiedene Stakeholder-Gruppen. Auch Aspekte wie bidirektionale vs. unidirektionale Kommunikation, Frequenz und Kosten des Kanalbespielens können so leicht berücksichtigt werden. Darüber hinaus können auch für die identifizierten Stakeholdergruppen konkrete Kommunikationsbedarfe erfasst werden.

Kommunikationsplan - Das zentrale Arbeitstool für Change Manager:

Hier werden alle Kommunikationsmaßnahmen mit Zielgruppen, Kanälen, Timing und Status geplant und können auf einer Zeitschiene visualisiert werden. Durch eine farbliche Markierung der Kommunikationsmaßnahmen wird sofort ersichtlich, ob es sich um bidirektionale Kommunikation (wie Mitarbeitermeetings) oder unidirektionale Kommunikation (wie Projektankündigungen) handelt und letztlich ein guter Kommunikationsmix geplant wurde.

Ist die App auch für Nicht-SAP-Projekte nutzbar?

Eine wichtige Frage aus der Community: Kann die App auch für andere Change-Projekte genutzt werden, etwa die Einführung eines CRM- oder TMS-Systems? Die Antwort ist eindeutig Ja. Stakeholder-Identifikation, Change Story Kommunikationskanäle – und bedarfe sowie der Kommunikationsplan können für jedes beliebige Change-Projekt genutzt werden. Der besondere Benefit für SAP-Projekte liegt in der Verknüpfung zu den SAP Business Best Practices.

Welche KI-Features sind geplant?

Bis Ende 2026 will SAP entscheiden, wo KI im Change Management-Bereich den maximalen Mehrwert bringt. Das langfristige Ziel ist ambitioniert: personalisierte Massenkommunikation für SAP-Projekte. Bei vielen betroffenen Mitarbeitenden soll die KI rollenspezifische Botschaften generieren, die nah an deren Business-Realität sind.

Wie soll das funktionieren?

Kundenspezifische Projektdokumente – PowerPoint-Präsentationen, Entscheidungsmemos, Scope-Dokumente – sollen in die App hochgeladen werden können. Darüber hinaus sollen Schnittstellen zu Signavio und CALM SAP-spezifischen Inhalt bereitstellen. Die KI extrahiert daraus die relevanten Change-Elemente für die Change Story: Was ist im Scope, warum wird das Projekt gemacht, was ist die Urgency, welche Vision wird verfolgt? Change Manager können dann darauf aufbauen und die Story mit Key Stakeholdern reflektieren und anpassen.

Weitere geplante Features sind die Ergänzung um Stakeholder-Analyse und Change Impact-Analyse – beides Kernaktivitäten eines Change Managers – sowie die automatische Generierung von rollenbasierten Kommunikationsplänen und -inhalten.

Wie wird Qualität sichergestellt?

Ein kritischer Aspekt wurde diskutiert: Die Change Story ist das Fundament der gesamten Change-Kommunikation und entsteht idealerweise im Dialog. Ist eine Tool-basierte Erstellung nicht problematisch?

Oliver Kohnke betonte: "Der Output muss reviewed werden. Es bleibt jedem Change Manager freigestellt, die Change Story mit wichtigen Stakeholdern, dem Project Sponsor oder im Projektteam zu reflektieren und anzupassen." Das Konzept des **"Human in the Loop"** ist zentral – die KI beschleunigt, ersetzt aber nicht die fachliche Bewertung.

Wie ist die Mehrsprachigkeit geregelt?

Bei internationalen Projekten ist Kommunikation in lokalen Sprachen entscheidend. Ist Mehrsprachigkeit verfügbar? Das Tool selbst ist momentan nur in Englisch verfügbar – dem Servicemodell geschuldet, um globale Skalierbarkeit zu ermöglichen.

Was kostet die App?

Die App ist über den SAP Micro-App Hub verfügbar. Die Foundational Features (Basisfunktionalitäten ohne AI) kosten 1.140 Euro pro Jahr für 5 Nutzer.

Die Lizenzen richten sich an Change Manager und Learning Manager – nicht an Endanwendende.

Wie funktioniert das Pricing für KI-Features?

Für AI-gestützte Funktionen gilt ein verbrauchsbasiertes Modell mit 7 Euro pro AI Unit**. Jede durch die KI generierte Seite Learning Content wird als "Page" gezählt. Bei der Generierung von 10.000 Pages innerhalb eines Monats würde der Monatspreis bei etwa 8.050 Euro liegen.

Welche Effizienzgewinne wurden bereits erreicht?

Die entscheidende Frage: Gibt es bereits Erfahrungen zu Produktivitätsgewinnen? Die Antwort ist beeindruckend: Ergebnisse aus dem Early Adoption Programm zeigten Effizienzgewinne von 50 bis 60 Prozent bei der AI-basierten Durchführung von Lernbedarfsanalysen und Generierung von Learninginhalten.

Fazit: Change Management wird messbar effizienter

Die Digitalisierung und Skalierung von Change Management ist keine Zukunftsvision mehr, sondern bereits Realität. SAP zeigt mit der neuen App einen pragmatischen Weg auf, wie Change Manager durch Standardisierung, Digitalisierung und KI-Unterstützung erhebliche Zeitgewinne erzielen können.

Die bereits erreichten Effizienzgewinne im Learning-Bereich zeigen auch das Potenzial für den Change Management-Bereich. Gleichzeitig bleibt die Herausforderung bestehen, Akzeptanz für diese digitalen Werkzeuge zu schaffen – genau jener Punkt, der auch in der Eingangsumfrage als größtes Hindernis identifiziert wurde.

Die Kombination aus bewährter Methodik, strukturierten Tools und intelligenter Automatisierung verspricht, Change Management in SAP-Projekten auf ein neues Effizienzniveau zu heben – ohne die notwendige menschliche Expertise und den Dialog mit Stakeholdern zu ersetzen.

Die nächste Kaffee-Ecke nach der Sommerpause widmet sich KI-Tools und Use Cases in der täglichen Arbeit – mit interaktivem Austausch in Kleingruppen statt großer Vorträge. 

Weiterführende Informationen:

  • Infos zu AI assisted User Learning and Change Management im SAP Micro-App Hub
  • Startseite SAP Activate Roadmap Viewer
  • OCM im SAP Activate Roadmap Viewer 
  • Alle SAP Lerninhalte auf learning.sap.com
  • OCM and Learning / Managing the People Side of SAP Cloud Projects
  • Kaffee-Ecke Event-Seite
  • Kontakt für Deep Dive-Sessions: oliver.kohnke@sap.com

entry.title: SAP Business Data Cloud (BDC) for LifeSciences & Healthcare: Building the Data Foundation for AI Era
entry.content:
The Data Challenge in Life Sciences and Healthcare Industry

Life Sciences and Healthcare organizations operate in one of the most data-intensive, highly regulated environments in the global economy. From clinical trial management and pharmacovigilance to patient outcome tracking and drug supply chain compliance, these organizations generate and consume massive volumes of data across disparate systems, geographies, and regulatory jurisdictions.

Yet despite significant investments in data and AI, many organizations struggle to realize transformative value. The reason is not a lack of data. It is a lack of shared business context that connects data to the processes, decisions, and outcomes that matter.

This challenge manifests as what SAP calls the complexity tax: multi-cloud sprawl, disconnected data across systems, and a critical absence of the semantic context that AI requires to deliver meaningful results. For an industry where a single regulatory non-compliance event can cost hundreds of millions, and where time-to-market for therapies directly impacts patient lives, this tax is not merely an IT inefficiency. It is a business risk.

Why Yesterday's Data Architecture Falls Short

Traditional data architectures were designed for a deterministic world. Optimized for applications and dashboards, they prioritized price-performance for structured reporting, predefined models, and static analytical outputs. This worked for generating quarterly reports and operational dashboards.

But Life Sciences and Healthcare now demand far more:

  • Clinical supply chain orchestration requires real-time data integration across manufacturing, logistics, regulatory, and partner systems.
  • Regulatory compliance (such as the U.S. Drug Supply Chain Security Act) requires end-to-end traceability and lineage across the entire product lifecycle.
  • Patient outcome analysis demands the ability to correlate data across electronic health records, clinical data warehouses, commercial operations, and real-world evidence sources.
  • AI-driven pharmacovigilance needs not just access to adverse event data, but deep understanding of how that data relates to manufacturing batches, distribution channels, and patient populations.

AI does not wait for insight; it acts on it. And acting requires continuous, context-rich understanding across systems, domains, and processes. The old architecture simply cannot deliver this.

SAP Business Data Cloud: A Data Fabric Built for the AI Era

SAP Business Data Cloud (BDC) represents a fundamental architectural shift. Rather than treating data as something to be moved, stored, and queried, BDC establishes a business data fabric where data is continuously connected to business context and made available for AI-driven decision-making and autonomous action.

The architecture consists of four integrated layers:

1. Lake Storage

BDC unifies SAP and non-SAP data across hybrid and cloud environments, creating a consistent foundation for both structured and unstructured data. For Life Sciences organizations, this means connecting data from SAP S/4HANA, SAP ECC, clinical trial systems, laboratory information management systems, and third-party data providers into a single accessible layer, without requiring costly data replication.

2. Intelligent Compute

Powered by SAP HANA Cloud, SAP Databricks, and SAP Snowflake, the intelligent compute layer provides flexible execution across diverse workloads. SAP HANA Cloud's multi-model processing (including graph, vector, spatial, and time-series capabilities) is particularly relevant for Life Sciences, where understanding relationships between molecules, patients, clinical sites, and regulatory requirements is fundamental to the business.

3. Knowledge Core

The Knowledge Core is where BDC fundamentally differentiates itself. It brings together:

  • Business semantics that define how metrics, KPIs, and data entities are interpreted consistently across the organization
  • Knowledge graphs that map relationships across processes and domains
  • Data products that deliver trusted, business-ready data
  • Analytics and simulations that enable scenario planning and trade-off analysis
  • Active metadata that captures how data is used, governed, and evolving

For Life Sciences and Healthcare, this means that when an AI agent evaluates a supply chain disruption, it does so with full understanding of regulatory requirements, batch genealogy, patient safety implications, and commercial commitments. Context is built into the system, not rebuilt for every use case.

4. AI Agents and Intelligent Applications

At the top of the architecture, SAP Joule orchestrates AI agents that turn context into autonomous action. These agents can execute multi-step, cross-application workflows grounded in business understanding. Purpose-built models trained on business data deliver domain-specific insights, while governance ensures every autonomous action remains traceable and compliant.

Data Products: The Building Blocks 

At the core of BDC's value proposition are data products: semantically-enriched, well-described, discoverable, and easily consumable data sets that serve as the fundamental building blocks for analytics and AI.

For Life Sciences and Healthcare, this means:

  • SAP-managed data products delivered out-of-the-box for common business domains (Finance, Supply Chain, Procurement, HR)
  • Customer-managed data products that extend and enrich the foundation with organization-specific clinical, regulatory, or commercial data including third-party data (market intelligence, real-world evidence, regulatory databases) into the same governed framework

SAP establishes a data product economy that serves multiple use cases through bidirectional, zero-copy Delta Share. Data products can be SAP-managed (delivered directly from SAP LoB source systems) or customer-managed (from customer-managed source systems, partners, or Databricks).

Currently available Data Products are listed on SAP Business Accelerator Hub. Upcoming Data Products and BDC product roadmap can be explored on SAP Road Map Explorer.

AI Agents: From Insight to Autonomous Action

SAP Business Data Cloud closes the loop between data and execution by providing ready-to-run insights with out-of-the-box analytical models, dashboards, and workflows. These are infused with AI/ML to generate recommendations and automate actions, curated and managed by SAP and SAP partners, and automatically connected to your data for accuracy and accelerated time to insights.

Context-aware agents are grounded in authoritative business context and governed metadata, eliminating silos between agents with a shared understanding of the business. SAP delivers intelligent content for each Line of Business (LoB):

  • Cloud ERP Intelligence (CFO) — Working Capital insights, Sustainability Control Tower
  • Finance Intelligence (CFO) — Cash Flow, General Ledger, Billing & Payments
  • People Intelligence (CHRO) — Workforce Composition, Skills, Compensation, Career Development
  • Spend Intelligence (CPO) — Spend Analysis & Insights, Invoices, Contracts, Purchase Orders
  • Supply Chain Intelligence (COO) — Logistics, Manufacturing, Inventory, Deliveries
  • Revenue Intelligence (CRO) — Customer 360, Customer Outreach Timing & Risk Signals

 

The Context Premium

SAP introduces the concept of the context premium: the competitive advantage gained by organizations that preserve and apply business context to their data and AI investments. In Life Sciences and Healthcare, this premium is particularly significant:

  • When AI agents share a unified understanding of the business, procurement decisions account for regulatory constraints, finance decisions account for clinical timelines, and compliance decisions account for commercial impact.
  • When data products carry semantic meaning, new AI use cases can be deployed without months of data preparation and context reconstruction.
  • When governance is embedded by design, regulatory audits become a matter of querying the system rather than assembling evidence from disconnected sources.

Business Capabilities and Getting Started

SAP Business Data Cloud enables organizations to move from fragmented data to a unified, AI-ready environment that drives both operational and strategic impact:

  • Simplify data landscapes across clouds
  • Establish automated governance and trust
  • Deliver business-ready data products with universal business context
  • Deploy context-aware agents and intelligent content
  • Optimize compute intelligently with FinOps stewardship
  • Transform outcomes with Joule Agents

Organizations can explore SAP Business Data Cloud through the product tour, free trial, Demo Hub, or a complimentary modernization assessment for SAP BW, BW/4HANA, BPC, BusinessObjects, and HANA environments.

Conclusion

For Life Sciences and Healthcare organizations, SAP Business Data Cloud is not merely a data platform upgrade. It is a strategic shift from managing data as a cost center to leveraging data as a continuously compounding business asset. By establishing a Knowledge Core that understands the unique complexity of regulated industries, embedding governance at every layer, and enabling AI agents to act with full business context, BDC addresses the fundamental challenge that has limited the return on data and AI investments across the industry.

The organizations that establish this foundation now will be the ones that achieve the context premium: faster drug development cycles, stronger regulatory postures, better patient outcomes, and more efficient commercial operations, all powered by AI that truly understands how their business operates.

entry.title: From pipelines to products- when your S/4HANA data becomes a product, not a pipeline.
entry.content:
What SAP actually changed: SAP becomes the producer

Data products are not new; the data-mesh community has argued for them since around 2019. What SAP did in Business Data Cloud (BDC) is structural: for SAP sources, SAP itself becomes the producer.

SAP-managed data products ship with the platform. They are pre-built extractions from S/4HANA and other SAP applications, landed and refreshed by SAP-managed pipelines into the BDC storage layer (HDLF), and published to the catalog with their business semantics attached. You do not build the extraction or its base semantics. You activate them, and build only the logic that is genuinely yours on top, which moves your scarce engineering effort from plumbing to differentiation. Where data-team bandwidth is the binding constraint, and for most organizations it is, that is the whole game.

This is not "only SAP data." There are three doors into the same governed catalog. Only the first is gated by an application purchase.

 

Why “zero-copy” is a fact, not a slogan

The foundation stores data products as open columnar files (Parquet, managed as Delta tables) in a managed object store built on SAP HANA Data Lake Files (HDLF). File-based is the key phrase. A warehouse locks data inside a proprietary format, so the only way to share it is to export a copy. An object store keeps data as open files in a bucket, and open files can be read by many engines at once without anyone copying anything.

This is the lakehouse principle applied inside SAP: one physical copy in open Delta format, read by SAP Datasphere, SAP Databricks, and SAP HANA Cloud through a shared catalog. Inside the BDC boundary, that means produce once and consume across all three engines without duplicating the data. One copy, many readers, that is the mechanism the market calls "zero-copy."

The full pipeline: how data actually lands in HDLF

For technical audiences, here is the honest end-to-end. CDS views are the extraction interface throughout, but the pipeline runs in two contexts. In the SAP-managed context, SAP owns standard, SAP-released CDS views built on the One Domain Model, its cross-application semantic model that keeps core entities consistent. In the customer-managed context, you create or identify the CDS entity yourself: a standard view, an extended or Z-CDS view, a custom business object, or a table. The same physical machinery moves both, and that machinery is what the rest of this section walks through.

 

 

 

Extraction at a glance, by source system

 

Cloud S/4HANA activates SAP-managed data products directly; everything else, on-prem S/4HANA, ECC, and non-SAP, enters through the customer-managed door. The source differs, the entry point does not: CDS views over the Cloud Connector for on-prem, extractors and tables via a Datasphere connection for ECC, database and file connections for third-party. Whatever the origin, one Replication Flow performs the universal physical write into HDLF as Delta Parquet, landing in a single governed catalog.

 

One product, end to end, and back

Follow a single product from source to intelligence and back. It leaves S/4HANA through SAP-managed CDS extraction and lands in the BDC foundation as governed Delta files. It appears in the catalog carrying its schema, semantics, and lineage: the "app store" moment where you browse before you install. The customer activates it, making it consumable.

From there the same single product serves everything. Datasphere models it; SAP Analytics Cloud plans and reports on it; and when a data-science team wants it, it is shared to SAP Databricks over Delta Sharing, appearing in Unity Catalog as a mounted table while the bytes stay in the foundation. What crosses the share is the table and its column-level meaning: names, types, keys, and comments, so the data scientist sees labeled, described columns rather than naked ones. What does not cross is the multidimensional model or the source-side access controls, measures, hierarchies, and data-access rules live in Datasphere and SAC, not in the shared file, and are re-established on the consumer side. Zero-copy is real at the boundary; meaning travels at the column level, not the model level.

When the loop closes, the data product is published back into BDC as a new governed data product through a federated remote share, live and zero-copy, not a physical import.

One honest boundary

Good advisory work separates what a platform will show from what it will guarantee. So, plainly: Delta Sharing transports tables, not multidimensional models. Measures, hierarchies, and data-access controls do not survive the protocol and must be re-established on the consuming side. The semantics that carry over are the structural ones (keys, column names, comments, data types), not the full analytic model. Say this up front and you will be trusted; let people discover it later and you will not.

Go Deeper On Delta Sharing: 

https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-sap/customer-managed-data-products-in-sap-bdc-what-really-happens-when-your/ba-p/14394675

The economics: what actually burns capacity

Most confusion about BDC cost comes from collapsing three things that are actually separate. Hold them apart and the economics stop being mysterious. This is the distinction solution architects and commercial teams have to be able to explain to a customer.

Illustrative split from a small worked example. Exact mix depends on data volume, refresh frequency, and workloads run.

The single most misunderstood point in the whole model: intelligent content does not consume capacity units. You license the application, but what burns capacity is the data products and infrastructure underneath it. Switching on more applications barely moves the meter. Cost follows the workloads you actually run on your data (provisioning, refresh, queries, and any optional ML in Databricks), not the number of applications you own.

Why this matters now

The reason the industry is talking about governed data products in 2026 is not nostalgia for data-mesh theory. It is AI. Agents and copilots fail in production mostly because they lack business context: they do not know that this table means settlement, or that this field is a posted invoice. A governed data product is that context, delivered with the data. The same foundation that ends your dashboard disagreements is what makes your S/4HANA data trustworthy enough to hand to an AI.

So the shift from pipelines to products is really two shifts at once. It retires a decade of brittle, unowned extractions and their hidden maintenance cost. And it builds the governed, meaningful foundation the next wave of enterprise AI actually requires, data that is AI-ready because it is governed and meaningful, not merely moved faster.

That is what changes when your S/4HANA data becomes a product, not a pipeline.

SAP Enterprise Architecture Designer SAP Datasphere SAP Databricks SAP Analytics Cloud, connectivity SAP Business Data Cloud

entry.title: Discover Autonomous Enterprise Innovations with SAP Road Map Explorer
entry.content:
Discover Autonomous Enterprise Innovations with SAP Road Map Explorer

As organizations accelerate their AI journeys, many are asking the same question: How can we turn AI innovation into measurable business outcomes?  SAP’s vision of the Autonomous Enterprise provides the answer with an enterprise where people set the direction, AI assists and coordinates, and intelligent agents execute routine work across business functions and industries.

To help customers explore this innovation journey, the SAP Road Map Explorer serves as a central destination for discovering current and planned Autonomous Enterprise innovations across SAP’s portfolio.

SAP Road Map Explorer makes these innovations transparent and accessible. You can explore how AI-powered assistants, agents, and capabilities are evolving across product areas and industries and you will understand not only what is available today, but also what is planned for future releases.

 

Finding Autonomous Enterprise Innovations in Road Map Explorer

SAP Road Map Explorer is designed as a self-service platform that presents current and planned innovations in a simple and consumable way.

For exploring the Road Map Priorities for the Autonomous Domains, you either access directly from the Landing Page or via the menu “Strategic Topics”.

 

 

No matter which option you choose, from here you start exploring the Autonomous Enterprise per the different Autonomous Domains:

 

If you prefer to get the "big picture” of all Autonomous Enterprise innovations in one screen, you search for the term “Autonomous Enterprise” in the search bar:

 

You will then receive a kanban board like view with all Autonomous Enterprise innovations sorted by quarters:

 

Turning Road Map Visibility into Strategic Conversations

SAP Road Map Explorer is more than a roadmap repository, it is a strategic engagement tool. You can save customized roadmaps, create collections of relevant innovations and share roadmap views with stakeholders.

By making innovations searchable, filterable, and easy to explore, SAP Road Map Explorer enables meaningful conversations about future transformation opportunities across both products and industries.

Ready to explore? Visit SAP Road Map Explorer, apply the relevant product and industry filters, and discover how Autonomous Enterprise innovations are shaping the future of business.

entry.title: Launching the APAC Edition of Architecting the Autonomous Enterprise - A 7 part Webinar Series
entry.content:
We are excited to announce the Asia Edition of the Architecting the Autonomous Enterprise webinar series.

A structured, seven-session programme designed specifically for architects who need to understand, evaluate, and act on SAP's Autonomous Enterprise strategy.  SAP's unique position (50 years of industry knowledge, the Knowledge Graph, semantically rich business data, and built-in governance) provides the structural foundation that generic AI approaches cannot replicate. But understanding how to architect for this shift requires more than reading documentation.

This series gives you direct access to the people building the platform, the reference architectures guiding decisions, and the practical patterns you need to translate strategy into delivery.

The Full Programme: Seven Sessions, One Architecture Journey

Register now: Register for the Series

 

124 August 2026Unpacking the Autonomous Enterprise: The What, Why, and How for ArchitectsSindhu Gangadharan
21 September 2026Architecting From Intent to Action: Joule Work and the Knowledge GraphRahul Lodhe, Sai Jaganath Remisetti
38 September 2026The Architect's Playbook for Building Your Own SAP AgentsJason Luo, Venkateshwaran Viswanathan, Gaurish Dessai
422 September 2026An Architect's Guide to AI Agent Lifecycle Management and the SAP Family of ModelsLaurenz Vogt, Mock Seng Chong
529 September 2026The AI North Star Architecture and AI Golden Path: From Reference to RealityPVN Pavankumar, Paul Craigie
66 October 2026Data Foundations for an Autonomous Enterprise: From Fragmented Systems to Trusted ContextNiclas Schautkotter, Alan Beven
713 October 2026The Role of Enterprise Architects in Driving the Autonomous EnterprisePaul Kurchina, Dirk Treusch, Johannes Euler, Alex Aitken

Session 1 | 24 August 2026

Unpacking the Autonomous Enterprise: The What, Why, and How for Architects
Speaker: Sindhu Gangadharan
The keynote session that sets the stage. Sindhu Gangadharan unpacks the vision, architecture, and practical implications of the Autonomous Enterprise, covering three context gaps (process, data, governance) that keep enterprise AI stuck and how SAP addresses each structurally. You will be introduced to the five building blocks: Joule and Joule Work, the SAP Autonomous Suite, Industry AI, the SAP Business AI Platform, and agent-led transformation.
Key Takeaways:
  • Understand the three context gaps and how SAP addresses each structurally
  • Map the five building blocks to your current landscape
  • Recognise what changes in your architecture practice: new UX patterns, domain maps, cross-domain orchestration, and governance as a first-class principle
Register for the Series

 

 Session 2 | 1 September 2026

 

Architecting From Intent to Action: Joule Work and the Knowledge Graph
Speakers: Rahul Lodhe, Sai Jaganath Remisetti
The way people interact with enterprise systems is fundamentally changing. Instead of navigating menus, filling forms, and manually connecting processes, people will express intent, and the system will orchestrate the response. This session covers how Joule Work introduces a dynamic engagement layer that interprets intent, learns from the Knowledge Graph, surfaces contextual insights, automates routine work, and orchestrates AI agents. The Knowledge Graph provides the semantic backbone: entity understanding, master data relationships, and business context that ground every agent interaction in meaning rather than guesswork.
Key Takeaways:
  • Understand the architecture of Joule Work as the unified engagement layer
  • Learn how the Knowledge Graph provides semantic and knowledge context: the difference between data and meaning for AI reasoning
  • Identify what your systems need to expose (APIs, events, semantic metadata) for Joule Work to operate effectively
Register for Session 2

 Session 3 | 8 September 2026

The Architect's Playbook for Building Your Own SAP Agents
Speakers: Jason Luo, Venkateshwaran Viswanathan, Gaurish Dessai
The SAP Business AI Platform provides a unified environment for building AI agents grounded in business context, but where do you start? What decisions define whether your agent works in a demo or in production? This session is a hands-on walkthrough of building an agent from scratch using the SAP stack: from Joule Studio and the SAP Integration Suite to the AI Core runtime. You will see the full development lifecycle: defining agent capabilities, grounding them in business data and process context, testing behaviour, and preparing for governed deployment.
Key Takeaways:
  • Walk through the end-to-end agent development lifecycle on the SAP Business AI Platform
  • Understand decision points: when to use deterministic applications vs. agents vs. agentic workflows vs. Knowledge Graph queries
Register for the Series

 Session 4 | 22 September 2026

An Architect's Guide to AI Agent Lifecycle Management and the SAP Family of Models
Speakers: Laurenz Vogt, Mock Seng Chong
As AI agents proliferate across enterprise landscapes, organisations face a defining architectural challenge: how do you discover, govern, secure, and measure the business impact of hundreds of autonomous agents? This session explores SAP's end-to-end approach to AI agent lifecycle management through the SAP AI Agent Hub (built on LeanIX), covering discovery and registration through governance, observability, and decommissioning. It also takes a deeper look at the SAP Family of Models, including RPT 1.5 which delivers 3.5x higher prediction quality than generic LLMs because it is pre-trained with business context.
Key Takeaways:
  • Understand the full agent lifecycle (discover, register, govern, observe, decommission) and how it maps to existing EA governance frameworks
  • Learn how the SAP AI Agent Hub on LeanIX connects agents to business capabilities, value streams, and organisational context
  • Learn what makes SAP's purpose-built models structurally different from generic LLMs
  • Identify the governance operating model needed before scaling from tens to hundreds of agents
Register for the Series

Session 5 | 29 September 2026

 

The AI North Star Architecture and AI Golden Path: From Reference to Reality
Speakers: PVN Pavankumar, Paul Craigie
Every architect navigating the Autonomous Enterprise asks the same question: what does the target architecture actually look like, and how do I get there from where I am today? The AI-Native North Star Architecture defines how SAP envisions the convergence of AI, data, and applications across the full stack. The AI Golden Path complements this with a guided journey from idea to production-ready application, including detailed decision trees for agents vs. deterministic applications vs. Knowledge Graph queries vs. HANA Vector Engine vs. agentic workflows vs. code-based agents. This session brings both to life with live demonstrations and worked examples.
Key Takeaways:
  • Understand the AI-Native North Star Architecture as the target state
  • Use the AI Golden Path decision framework for consistent build-vs-buy and pattern-selection decisions
  • See live demonstrations of how reference patterns translate into working implementations
  • Learn how to use published assets from the SAP Architecture Center as alignment tools with stakeholders
Register for the Series

 Session 6 | 6 October 2026

Data Foundations for an Autonomous Enterprise: From Fragmented Systems to Trusted Context
Speakers: Niclas Schautkotter, Alan Beven
The difference between isolated AI pilots and sustainable AI at scale lies in the foundational data architecture. Without trusted, connected, contextual data, agents hallucinate, predictions fail, and governance becomes impossible. This session covers how SAP brings data and AI together with a deep dive into SAP Business Data Cloud, including zero-copy data extraction, delta share protocol, BW modernisation, and the evolving role of the Data Architect in an autonomous landscape.
Key Takeaways:
  • Understand why Data Context is the foundational layer of the Context System
  • Learn the architecture of SAP Business Data Cloud and BDC Connect, including zero-copy patterns
  • Understand the evolving role of the Data Architect: from ETL designer to context curator for AI agents
Register for the Series

Session 7 | 13 October 2026

 

The Role of Enterprise Architects in Driving the Autonomous Enterprise
Speakers: Paul Kurchina, Dirk Treusch, Johannes Euler, Alex Aitken
The closing session that brings it all together. When AI agents execute processes, orchestrate across domains, and make decisions grounded in business context, the architect's role shifts from system design to shaping the operating model, governance boundaries, and transformation roadmap. This session explores what the Autonomous Enterprise means for Enterprise Architects: how decision-making changes, what new competencies become essential, and how EAs position themselves as strategic connectors between business ambition and AI-enabled execution. Includes a complete series wrap-up and clear next steps.
Key Takeaways:
  • Understand how the EA role evolves: from system integrator to transformation orchestrator and governance steward
  • Learn new competency areas: agent lifecycle governance, context architecture design, cross-domain orchestration, AI-native operating models
  • Take away a consolidated view of the series: key patterns, architectural decisions, and reusable assets
  • Connect with a community of architects navigating the same transition
Register for the Series

 Who Should Attend

This series is designed for:
  • Enterprise Architects evaluating AI-native operating models and governance frameworks
  • Solution Architects designing agent-based solutions on the SAP stack
  • Data Architects building the contextual data foundations that AI agents depend on
  • Technology Leaders responsible for AI strategy and platform decisions across Asia-Pacific
Whether you are early in your AI journey or already building agents in production, each session provides actionable architectural guidance grounded in real platform capabilities.
 
All sessions run at 11:30 AM IST | 14:00 SGT | 16:00 AEST | 08:00 CEST, designed for Asia-Pacific time zones with accessibility for colleagues in Europe.
Registration is open now and is completely free for everyone!
 
Register for all sessions: url.sap/aewebinar-apac
 
Each session is independent, but the series is designed as a progressive architecture journey. Attending all seven sessions will give you the complete picture. Select the sessions relevant to your role, or join the full series to build a comprehensive understanding of what the Autonomous Enterprise means for your architecture practice.

entry.title: The Octopus Brain
entry.content:
An octopus has around 500 million neurons in a massive nervous system. Where humans concentrate most neurons in the head, an octopus does the opposite. About two-thirds live in the arms themselves — roughly 40 million per arm — forming eight semi-autonomous mini-brains. Each arm tastes, touches, and makes motor decisions on its own. A central brain coordinates.
That's the architecture I ended up building — accidentally, then deliberately — for the way Claude works with my knowledge. Hence: Project Octopus. Here's what broke, what fixed it, and a prompt you can borrow to try the same pattern on your own knowledge bases.

The problem: four brains, no conversation

The whole thing started after I worked through Ken Lomax's wonderful workshop, My Second Brain (you find similar content on Youtube) — the idea that you have your own wiki which Claude Code builds from a folder with raw files. The idea landed, and I got to work creating brains one at a time.

First I had just the one that helps me with organizing my tool knowledge, mostly in the unfathomable depths of ABAP documentation. Realizing that I cannot have one large brain for all my needs, I started to create other ones, to avoid dilution of knowledge. I created another one for the new field of code-based agents, and one for personal development which became a pretty cool career advisor as we are currently preparing our Q3 SAP talks. And another one for API and Business Object documentation.

Keeping them separate was intentional. A single mega-brain collapses under its own weight, mixing goal-setting advice with ABAP transaction quirks.

The trade-off: Claude only knew about the individual ones when you worked in the corresponding project folder. Sit inside one brain and drift into a topic that belongs to another, and Claude has no way of knowing the other brain even exists. The answer comes from raw model knowledge — plausible, generic, and sometimes wrong.

The fix: a coordinating brain

The solution turned out to be smaller than expected. Not a fifth brain, but a compact registry — one table in the global CLAUDE.md that every Claude session loads automatically. Each row names a brain, its path, its scope, and the topics that trigger a consult. Two rules follow: read the matching brain's index before answering, and reconcile across brains when a topic straddles two of them.

That's it. No new folders, no orchestration layer, no third-party tooling. Just a lookup table that lives where Claude already looks.

The octopus metaphor writes itself. The four brains are the arms — specialized, autonomous, each holding its own hard-won knowledge. The registry is the central brain, unimpressive on paper, doing the one thing central brains do well: routing signals to the arm that already knows the answer.

 

Try it yourself

If you have two or more brains and want the same routing setup, drop the following into a Claude session and let it do the discovery and wiring for you.

Note: Your brain folders might not be called `mybrain<Name>` — adapt the naming in the prompt below to match your own convention.

Here's the prompt:

I have multiple "second brain" folders on my machine — each is a folder named `mybrain<Something>` following the LLM-Wiki pattern, with `raw/`, `wiki/`, and a `CLAUDE.md` at the root. Right now Claude only sees whichever brain I happen to be sitting in, so knowledge in the other brains stays invisible for the session. Fix that. Steps:

1. **Find all my brains.** Search my user home directory and its subfolders (including any OneDrive folders) for directories matching `mybrain*` that contain a `CLAUDE.md` at their root. Skip `.zip` archives, empty folders, and staging folders that lack the full brain structure.

2. **Read each brain's `CLAUDE.md`** to pull out its purpose and scope. Do not modify anything inside the brain folders.

3. **Locate my global Claude config** at `~/.claude/CLAUDE.md` (Windows: `C:\Users\<me>\.claude\CLAUDE.md`). If it does not exist, stop and tell me before creating anything.

4. **Prepare a new section** titled `## Personal Knowledge Bases (mybrain* wikis)` containing:
- A one-line introduction to the routing pattern.
- A markdown table with one row per brain, columns: **Brain**, **Path**, **Scope**, **Consult when**. Derive "Consult when" from each brain's own scope, pulling out two or three concrete topic keywords.
- A short workflow: read the matching brain's `wiki/index.md` first, cite pages, reconcile across brains when a topic spans two of them, fall back to model knowledge only when no brain covers the topic.
- A rule that each brain's `raw/` folder is read-only.

5. **Show me the preview** and ask me to confirm before writing the section into the global CLAUDE.md. Preserve everything already in that file — append or splice in cleanly, do not overwrite unrelated sections.

6. **After writing**, tell me how to verify: open a new Claude session, ask a question that clearly belongs to one specific brain from any folder on disk, and confirm Claude routes to that brain's wiki before answering.

Do not create any new brain folders. Do not modify anything inside the existing brains. Only edit the global `~/.claude/CLAUDE.md`.

Closing thoughts

That's the whole pattern: four arms of specialized knowledge, one small table in the middle, no orchestration layer. Total wiring cost was one section in a markdown file, and Claude now routes across brains without me having to remember which folder I'm sitting in.

If you're running multiple wikis and want the same routing, the prompt above will set it up for you. And if you've built a different way of stitching separate knowledge bases together, drop it in the comments — I'd like to see what other shapes this pattern can take.

entry.title: Skin in the robotic game
entry.content:
The effort to create humanoid robots does not stop at the mechanical re-engineering of human mechanics. With natural language capabilities rapidly evolving, computers can engage in lively conversations which use – if they are online – the capabilities of resources like large language models. Think talking to OpenAI's GPT-Realtime via a humanoid robot.

While this is easy to explain, it is as well observable that the interactions with (humanoid) robots of today do not make use of these capabilities – but I have only seen a few, and the legal frameworks governing the access to these tools will certainly have an impact.

Voice interaction is a good example of the convergence of technology. Another striking example is that companies are bringing skin into the game: robots with faces and skin which feels soft to the touch.

As I've been engaged with robots and humanoids for some time now, I may be through what is called the "uncanny valley" already, and I would love to hear what your first reaction is when you look at the video I add to this blog post.

The uncanny valley is the uncomfortable feeling associated with machines becoming awkwardly human. While criticism is voiced over the exact curve of the valley, there is unanimous agreement that robots which become "too human" but are still identifiable as robots cause discomfort. My personal experience is that this goes away once you clearly tag them as robots and don't get caught in the initial zone of not being sure whether it's a human or a machine anymore.

 

When it comes to business, my first thought is that equipping robots with real skin (and up-to-speed voice interaction) is an amazing opportunity for gaming and entertainment parks. Imagine walking into a venue like a museum, where you could get to a new level of interactivity with historical characters – or simply create engaging role play scenarios (LARP) with non-player characters.

I'm focusing on gaming here because my personal perspective is that putting "realistic" robots in front of humans for interactions around their personal life is a real danger. Already today, people engage with computers as if they were talking to humans, and they did so since ELIZA. Adding skin to the game will further increase the risk of not being able to distinguish whether you engage with a human or not – and that risk is not that you treat a robot like you might have treated your favorite stuffed toy as a child. The risk is that this stuffed toy has access to almost unlimited information, might be able to analyze you better than any human, and may have been programmed (or: prompted) unknowingly to you to pursue an influencing agenda which you are not aware of.

That's why I put gaming forward, where the roles are clear – but let me know in the comments where you would see lifelike, skinned robots in application?

entry.title: From Whiteboard to SAP BTP: Building an Open Source AI Architecture Diagram Generator
entry.content:
Every SAP BTP architect knows the ritual. You finish a whiteboard session, photograph the sketch on your phone, and then spend the next hour manually recreating it in draw.io — hunting through the SAP icon library, wiring up the right service boxes, getting the boundary zones right. The diagram ends up taking longer than the actual architecture decision.

We built a tool to fix that. Sketch to SAP BTP takes a whiteboard photo or a Mermaid diagram and converts it into an official SAP BTP Solution Diagram in .drawio format — complete with the right SAP service icons, boundary zones, and connector styles. It runs in seconds, on your own BTP subaccount, using your own Anthropic API key.

Today we're releasing it as open source under Apache 2.0.

The tool UI — file upload tab and Mermaid input tab side by side

What it does

The tool accepts two kinds of input:

  • Whiteboard sketch — a photo (PNG, JPEG, WebP, GIF up to 10 MB) of a hand-drawn architecture
  • Mermaid diagram — any flowchart or graph definition

From either input, it runs a four-step pipeline:

Identify components → Map to SAP catalog → Review architecture → Generate .drawio

The output is a ready-to-open .drawio file with proper SAP service icons, swimlane boundary zones, and styled connectors. Drop it into diagrams.net or the draw.io desktop app and it opens correctly without any manual adjustment.

The technical implementation

Step 1 — Claude Vision extracts the architecture

For sketch input, we use Claude's vision capability with structured tool use. Rather than asking Claude to describe the image in natural language and then parsing that output, we define a tool schema that forces the model to return a structured JSON representation of the architecture directly.

// srv/agents/vision.js — simplified
const tools = [{
  name: "extract_architecture",
  input_schema: {
    type: "object",
    properties: {
      title: { type: "string" },
      components: {
        type: "array",
        items: {
          type: "object",
          properties: {
            id: { type: "string" },
            label: { type: "string" },
            type: { type: "string" }
          }
        }
      },
      connections: { /* ... */ }
    }
  }
}];

This approach eliminates prompt-parsing fragility. The model either calls the tool with valid structured data or it doesn't — there's no regex or text parsing required on our side. Claude Sonnet 4.6 with vision handles even rough whiteboard photos reliably.

A whiteboard sketch being uploaded

Step 2 — Mermaid gets a dedicated parser (no LLM needed)

When the input is a Mermaid diagram, we skip the vision model entirely. Mermaid is structured text — parsing it deterministically is faster, cheaper, and more accurate than asking an LLM to interpret it. Our parser handles:

  • All flowchart directions: LR, TD, RL, BT
  • Edge types: -->, -->|label|, -.->, ==>
  • Chained edges: A --> B --> C
  • Cylinder shape [(label)] for databases
  • YAML front-matter and %% title: for diagram titles

Mermaid parsing costs exactly $0 and returns results in milliseconds.

Step 3 — Deterministic SAP service catalog mapping

Once we have a list of labelled components, we map each one to an official SAP BTP service using sap_service_catalog.json — a bundled catalog of SAP services with their draw.io style metadata. Matching runs in three tiers:

Mapping confidence tiers Exact match — the label directly matches a service name or alias (e.g. "SAP HANA Cloud" → hana-cloud)
Alias match — a known abbreviation or alternative name matches (e.g. "HANA" → hana-cloud)
Type fallback — no match found, but the component type (database, queue, API) suggests a generic placeholder

This is all deterministic code — no LLM involved in the mapping step. It's fast, predictable, and easy to extend by adding entries to sap_service_catalog.json.

Step 4 — Architecture review with Claude Haiku

Before generating the final diagram, we pass the mapped architecture to Claude Haiku 4.5 for a lightweight best-practices review. It checks for common issues — missing integration layers, direct database access without a service tier, absent authentication components — and returns structured warnings.

We use Haiku for this step deliberately: the review prompt is short and the expected output is structured JSON warnings. Using a smaller model here keeps the cost per run under a cent while still catching real architectural issues.

The validation results are available two ways: embedded in the downloaded .drawio response as HTTP headers (X-Validation-Summary, X-Warning-Count, X-Missing-Services), or as a standalone JSON response via POST /validate-only if you want the review without generating a diagram.

The validation panel showing detected SAP components with confidence chips and architecture review warnings

Step 5 — draw.io XML generation

The final step converts the mapped, validated architecture into draw.io XML. SAP publishes an official icon set for BTP services — we bundle these as base64 SVG in sap_icon_index.json and embed them directly into the draw.io cell styles. The generator places components in boundary zones (Integration Suite, Extension Suite, etc.) based on service category, positions them using a topology-aware BFS layout — components are assigned columns based on their graph depth from actor/entry-point nodes, preserving the left-to-right data flow of the original sketch — and wires up connectors.

The output is standard draw.io XML — it opens in diagrams.net, the draw.io desktop app, or any tool that reads the format.

The generated .drawio file open in diagrams.net showing SAP service icons, boundary zones, and connectors

API key resolution — three sources, one priority order

The app resolves the Anthropic API key from these sources in priority order:

Source When used
VCAP_SERVICES.aicoreSAP AI Core / GenAI Hub binding
VCAP_SERVICES.credential-storeBTP Credential Store — recommended for production
ANTHROPIC_API_KEY env varLocal dev and quick CF deployments

This means the tool works with SAP's own AI Core infrastructure for organisations already using GenAI Hub, while remaining simple to run locally with a direct API key.

Cost per run

Step Model Approx. cost
Sketch analysisclaude-sonnet-4-6~$0.013
Architecture reviewclaude-haiku-4-5~$0.003
Mermaid parsingNo LLM$0.00

All costs are charged to your own Anthropic account — Aditheos has zero visibility into your usage or spend.

Why we open sourced it

The honest answer: a commercial SaaS model doesn't fit this tool well.

Enterprise SAP architects handle sensitive network topology and system landscape data. They will deploy an open source tool to their own private BTP space without hesitation. Getting the same data approved to flow through an external SaaS is a legal review and a procurement process. The paywall would block the exact people the tool is most useful to.

There's also the catalog maintenance problem. SAP's BTP service catalog changes constantly — new services, renamed services, deprecated plans. If this were a closed product, we'd be solely responsible for keeping sap_service_catalog.json up to date. Open source means the community can submit pull requests when a service launches or changes. That's a better outcome for everyone.

The most impactful contribution Adding new entries to data/sap_service_catalog.json — service names, aliases, draw.io style metadata — directly improves mapping accuracy for every user. If your organisation uses a service that isn't mapping correctly, a PR to the catalog is the highest-leverage fix.

Getting started in under five minutes

Local development:

git clone https://github.com/aditheos/sketch-to-sap-btp-cap.git
cd sketch-to-sap-btp-cap

cp .env.example .env
# Add your ANTHROPIC_API_KEY to .env

npm install
npm run dev   # → http://localhost:4004

Deploy to your own BTP subaccount:

npm install
npm run build

cf login -a <your-api-endpoint>
cf target -o <your-org> -s <your-space>
cf deploy sketch-to-sap-btp_*.mtar

cf set-env sketch-to-sap-btp-srv ANTHROPIC_API_KEY sk-ant-...
cf restage sketch-to-sap-btp-srv

No additional BTP services required. The deployment is a single Cloud Foundry app. No HANA, no approuter, no IAS — just the CAP backend serving the UI and handling conversions.

What's next

A few directions the community could take this:

  • SAP BAS extension — a plugin that runs the conversion inside Business Application Studio, where architects actually spend their time
  • Two-way generation — from diagram to CAP/BTP project scaffold (the reverse direction)
  • More output formats — PowerPoint-ready slides, Confluence diagrams, PlantUML
  • Expanded catalog — deeper coverage of SAP Integration Suite, SAP Build, and industry-specific services

The repository is at github.com/aditheos/sketch-to-sap-btp-cap. Issues and pull requests are open
Source Reference: Sketch to SAP BTP Diagram

entry.title: Building and Deploying Your First MCP Server on SAP BTP
entry.content:
1. Understanding MCP

The Model Context Protocol (MCP) is a standard that lets an AI model call external tools and read data in a structured way. Think of it like a USB standard for AI — just as USB lets any device plug into any computer, MCP lets any tool plug into any AI model.

The Three Primitives

An MCP server can expose three types of capabilities:

Primitive

Purpose

Analogy

Tools

Functions the AI can call and execute — like pressing a button

A menu item in a restaurant

Resources

Data the AI can read as context — like a reference document

A reference manual

Prompts

Reusable prompt templates the AI or user can invoke

A saved workflow

In this tutorial we focus on Tools — the most powerful primitive for SAP integration.

Architecture

The flow is simple:

 

 

 

 

 

The user types a natural language request. The AI reads the tool descriptions (docstrings), decides which tool to call, the MCP server executes the OData call, and returns the formatted result to the AI — which then replies to the user.

2. Prerequisites

Before we start, ensure you have the following installed and configured:

Requirement

Version

Purpose

Python

3.10+

Runtime for the MCP server

VS Code

Latest

IDE for development and debugging

Node.js

18+

Required for the MCP Inspector browser UI

CF CLI

v8+

Deploy to SAP BTP Cloud Foundry

SAP BTP Account

—

Cloud Foundry environment with a space

Install the VS Code Python extension from the Extensions marketplace (Ctrl+Shift+X → search Python → Install).

3. Setting Up VS Code

Step 1 — Create the Project Folder

Open VS Code and go to File → Open Folder. Create a new folder called my_first_mcp and open it. Your VS Code Explorer should show MY_FIRST_MCP at the top.

Step 2 — Open the Terminal

Go to Terminal → New Terminal. A terminal panel opens at the bottom of VS Code.

Step 3 — Create a Virtual Environment

A virtual environment isolates your project's Python packages from the rest of your system — each project has its own clean set of dependencies.

python3 -m venv .venv --upgrade-deps

Activate it:

Mac / Linux:

source .venv/bin/activate

Windows:

.venv\Scripts\activate

Your terminal prompt changes to show (.venv) — this confirms the environment is active.

VS Code may prompt 'Do you want to select this environment as the workspace interpreter?' — click Yes. This ensures the debugger uses the correct Python.

Step 4 — Install Dependencies

pip install "mcp[cli]" httpx

This installs two packages:

  • mcp[cli] — FastMCP server and the mcp dev Inspector command
  • httpx — an async HTTP client for making OData calls

Verify the installation:

mcp version

You should see the mcp version number printed.

4. Building the MCP Server

Create a new file in your project folder called server_trippin.py. This will be our complete MCP server.

Step 1 — The Server Skeleton

Every MCP server starts with the same three lines:

import httpx

from mcp.server.fastmcp import FastMCP

mcp = FastMCP("TripPin Server")

BASE_URL = "https://services.odata.org/TripPinRESTierService"

FastMCP is the high-level framework that handles the MCP protocol for us. We give the server a name and set the base URL for our OData service.

Step 2 — Understanding the Tool Pattern

Every tool follows the same pattern:

@mcp.tool()

async def tool_name(parameter: type) -> str:

    """Docstring — the AI reads this to know when to use this tool."""

    # make HTTP call

    # parse response

    # return formatted string

 

Part

What it does

@mcp.tool()

Registers this function as an MCP tool

Function name

Becomes the tool name the AI calls

Parameters + types

Become the tool's input schema automatically

Docstring

The AI reads this to decide when to use the tool

Return value

Sent back to the AI as the tool result

Step 3 — The GET Tools (Read Operations)

Let's add our first two tools — listing and fetching people:

@mcp.tool()

async def get_people(top: int = 5) -> str:

    """Get a list of people. top = how many to return (default 5)."""

    url = f"{BASE_URL}/People"

    params = {"$format": "json", "$top": top,

              "$select": "UserName,FirstName,LastName,Gender"}

    async with httpx.AsyncClient() as client:

        response = await client.get(url, params=params)
